angle: Best-in-class operator-tool craft: what premium B2B/operator dashboards do at the moments of trust, speed, and glanceability — and precisely how Switchboard closes the gap between its editorial ceiling and its hobbyist floor.

principles.principle: Optimistic-first, undo-as-safety-net: update the UI before the server responds, then reconcile. Confirmation dialogs are wrong in 90% of cases — undo is the correct safety net for reversible actions.
principles.why: Superhuman targets 50–60ms perceived response times. Linear's local-first sync eliminates spinners entirely because there is nothing to wait for — the UI re-renders off in-memory state synchronously. For reversible decisions (approve a marketing recommendation, decline a low-risk comms draft), a countdown-gated undo is strictly superior to a modal interrupt: it doesn't break flow, assumes the operator knows what they are doing, and is less fatiguing. Confirmations should be reserved for genuinely irreversible, money-committing actions.
principles.source: Blake Crosley / Superhuman design study (blakecrosley.com/en/guides/design/superhuman); performance.dev Linear breakdown; Smashing Magazine 'How To Manage Dangerous Actions' (Sep 2024)

principles.principle: The card must leave before the network responds. Freeze = loss of trust. EXIT_MS should be at most 150ms using GPU-only properties (transform/opacity); never block removal on a pending fetch.
principles.why: Linear's asymmetric timing — appear instantly, dismiss in ~150ms — keeps the critical path (user action → feedback) below the 100ms psychological threshold where interaction feels instantaneous. Animating layout-triggering properties (height, margin) causes jank. The card leaving IS the confirmation; the undo is the hedge. Switchboard's current 320ms CSS transition + racing EXIT_MS=280 is the inverse: a pause at the worst moment.
principles.source: performance.dev/how-is-linear-so-fast-a-technical-breakdown; Vercel design guidelines (vercel.com/design/guidelines)

principles.principle: Skeleton before spinner, always. Skeletons must be layout-matched to the real content — same column widths, same card heights. Generic grey bars feel like a broken page; matched skeletons feel like a page arriving.
principles.why: Layout-matched skeletons reduce perceived load time by up to 40% versus spinners. The premium signal is that the skeleton is clearly the shape of something the operator already knows, not a placeholder that will rearrange on reveal. Vercel's Geist guidelines are explicit: skeletons must be 'stable' — mirroring final content exactly to avoid layout shift. Add a 150–300ms show-delay to prevent flicker on fast responses.
principles.source: Vercel Geist skeleton guidelines (vercel.com/geist/skeleton); LogRocket 'Skeleton loading screen design'; 6 Premium Loading State Patterns (UX World/Medium)

principles.principle: Design the commit moment as a 'before-and-after receipt', not a gate. Show what changes, what is at risk, and what is recoverable — at the moment of action, not in a nested settings screen.
principles.why: A wire transfer confirmation screen with a one-second deliberate delay and a clear summary is now a recognized trust mechanism in fintech — a signal that the system is being appropriately careful. Stripe's gold standard is showing the exact amount, fee, and destination before commit. For an AI revenue agent spending an operator's ad budget, the 'What this changes / Money at risk' panel is the most important screen in the product. Shipping it with four em-dashes destroys the trust the rest of the product builds. The minimum honest panel requires only the already-available fields: action type, estimated cost or customer impact, the agent's confidence, and the undoable-until window.
principles.source: Stripe payment UX analysis; Onething Design 'Top 10 Fintech UX Practices 2026'; Smashing Magazine destructive-actions patterns (2024)

principles.principle: Stale state is a lie. The primary action path must invalidate all downstream counts immediately. If approving an item on the main Inbox does not remove it from the feed AND decrement 'things need you', the operator cannot trust their own numbers.
principles.why: This is a correctness principle masquerading as a UX principle. Superhuman's archive is optimistic — the email is gone before the server confirms. Linear's issue close removes the item from the queue before sync completes. An operator who clears three items and sees the counter still say five stops trusting the product's understanding of their work. The query invalidation contract (decisions.feed + needs-you count + per-agent filter counts) must be a single, coordinated onMutate + onSettled pair — not split across the drawer path vs the main page.
principles.source: Superhuman design study; SWR/React Query optimistic update patterns; Vitaly Friedman toast UX analysis (LinkedIn)

principles.principle: One token system, enforced by tests. Each agent identity color must be defined exactly once and consumed everywhere as hsl(var(--agent-x)). Drift between the hardcoded-hex T object and CSS vars means any color fix must be made 3+ times and will still regress.
principles.why: The W3C Design Tokens Format Module (2024) and every major design system (Atlassian, Vercel Geist, Carbon) converge on the same rule: a single source of truth for design decisions, with platform outputs generated from it. mira-config.ts already demonstrates the var() pattern is viable. The migration cost is bounded: the T cockpit object has ~18 values; the live footprint (after removing dead ApprovalCard/roi-bar) is smaller. The unlock is that dark mode, agent rebrand, and all WCAG color fixes become single-source changes. tokens.test.ts already exists to host the drift guard.
principles.source: W3C Design Tokens Format Module; Atlassian migrate-to-tokens; Vercel Geist (vercel.com/design/guidelines); penpot.app token guide

principles.principle: Restraint is the premium signal. Not every element deserves equal visual weight. Reduce chrome, dim navigation, let content lead. 'If most people don't immediately notice what changed, that's probably a good sign.'
principles.why: Linear's 2024 design refresh deliberately dimmed the sidebar and reduced icon density so the main content area dominates. The principle: structure should be felt, not seen. Borders and dividers replaced by spatial hierarchy. For Switchboard, where the entire nav chrome competes with the operator's inbox of pending decisions, reducing the weight of everything that is not an action item directly increases the glanceability of what demands attention.
principles.source: Linear 'A calmer interface for a product in motion' (linear.app/now/behind-the-latest-design-refresh); Karri Saarinen 10 Rules (figma.com/blog)

principles.principle: Toasts fail as the primary undo surface for sequential approvals. Update the component's state in-place, or use a sticky/persistent band that queues — never a TOAST_LIMIT=1 that evicts the prior undo on the next approval.
principles.why: Vitaly Friedman's toast UX analysis is direct: toasts 'often appear far away from the point of interaction', fail WCAG 2.2.1 Timing Adjustable, don't receive focus, and are hard to use via keyboard. For a sequential approval queue where an operator approves multiple items in rapid succession, TOAST_LIMIT=1 silently destroys the undo for every item except the last. The correct pattern for this context is either inline state change on the card (a reversed/undone state where the card re-appears), or a persistent bottom-bar that queues undo actions with individual countdown timers.
principles.source: Vitaly Friedman 'Toasts and Snackbars UX Guidelines' (LinkedIn); Smashing Magazine dangerous-actions Sep 2024

principles.principle: Keyboard triage is not a power-user feature on a decision-feed product — it is the core interaction model for a desktop operator doing their daily review. J/K navigation, E to approve, R to request changes, Esc to dismiss must exist at launch.
principles.why: Superhuman's central thesis is that keyboard navigation compounds: time saved on 100 daily approvals is existentially different from time saved on 10. The decision-to-action loop in Inbox — where the operator clears the morning queue before their first client arrives — is structurally identical to Superhuman's inbox-zero flow. The cmd+K command palette is also standard for premium B2B tools (Linear, Ramp, Height all have it); its absence in a product that describes itself as an 'operating system' is a credibility gap.
principles.source: Superhuman design study; Ramp CMD+K review (efficient.app); Height keyboard shortcuts review; Linear keyboard first design

principles.principle: The emptiness of a calm day must not feel like the emptiness of a broken page. Design two distinct empty-state registers: 'first-run activation' (guide + CTA) and 'all-clear earned' (celebratory, brief, warm). Never use 'No active handoffs' as a label for a hardcoded empty array.
principles.why: Eleken's empty-state analysis identifies the clearest premium vs. broken signal: 'Completely blank screens with no messaging' and 'Nothing here yet' feel broken; a direct, context-aware explanation with a single CTA feels premium. Slack's 'All caught up' and Linear's simple monochrome illustrations are canonical. For Switchboard, a calm day should feel like winning ('Alex handled 12 enquiries while you slept — nothing needs you today') not system failure ('0 open enquiries').
principles.source: Eleken 'Empty state UX examples' (eleken.co); UserOnboard empty states patterns; Carbon Design System empty-states

principles.principle: Dark mode is a shipped liability until the full token cascade is var()-based. The correct decision is to disable the Dark/System toggle at launch and complete the ~105 missing dark tokens in a follow-up sprint, not ship a mode that auto-triggers on dark-OS phones and breaks 70% of surfaces.
principles.why: The medspa operator audience is mobile-first. System dark mode auto-applies on first load, no user action required. Breaking all editorial tokens, all agent identity tokens, all cockpit hex values, and Mercury at once — on first open — is a credibility-destroying first impression. Vercel's pure-black dark mode works because it was designed as a coherent system from the start; Switchboard's dark mode was layered onto a system that was never built for it. Disabling it is a one-line change; completing it correctly is a multi-sprint project. Ship clean.
principles.source: Vercel design philosophy; CSS variables dark mode best practices (webtoolshub.online); use-theme.ts verified behavior

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Local-first sync means the UI re-renders synchronously off in-memory state with zero spinners. Their 2024 design refresh explicitly reduced icon density, dimmed navigation chrome, softened structural borders ('structure should be felt, not seen'), and shifted to warmer grays — making the content area feel dominant. Their command palette (Cmd+K) backed by in-memory data is faster than a search. Asymmetric animation timing: appear instantly, exit in ~150ms using only transform/opacity. Karri Saarinen's 10 rules codify taste-driven craft over A/B testing: small teams, specs as baselines not goals, reduce scope to increase quality.
exemplars.takeaway: The card-leaves-before-the-server-responds interaction model is achievable with React Query optimistic updates without a full sync engine. The 'structure felt not seen' refresh is directly applicable to Switchboard's nav chrome vs. content competition. Commit to matching Switchboard's Inbox card exit to ≤150ms GPU-only animation.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: 50–60ms internal target for all interactions — the product is built around the principle that speed is the product, not a feature. Optimistic archiving: E key removes the email, focus moves to next, server sync is background. Undo (Z) is the safety net, not a confirm modal. J/K vim-style spatial navigation. Command palette displays keyboard shortcut alongside every action name, training muscle memory passively. Onboarding drills shortcuts in a synthetic inbox before touching the real one.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Inbox is structurally identical to Superhuman's inbox: a queue of items to triage daily. The J/K/E/R/Esc keyboard model transfers directly. The 'undo as safety net, not confirm as gate' principle should govern every low-risk approval in the decision feed. The cmd+K palette — backed by cached decision data — makes the operator feel expert within days.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: The gold standard for money-adjacent confirmation: before committing any payment, the operator sees the exact amount, fee, destination, and timing. Inline real-time validation (card type detection, format feedback) rather than post-submit errors. Clear success/failure states with receipts. The dashboard surfaces exactly when funds hit the bank — no ambiguity about what is settled vs. pending. Error messages explain the solution, not just the problem: 'Your API key is incorrect or expired. Generate a new key in account settings.'
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's 'What this changes / Money at risk: —' panel is the anti-pattern of Stripe's confirmation standard. The fix is not wiring a complex preview system — it is showing the fields already available in the Decision payload (action type, estimated spend, affected customer, agent confidence, undoable-until) with honest em-dash fallbacks only for fields genuinely unavailable. Solution-first error copy ('This approval was already handled by [teammate] 4m ago — nothing to do') should replace silent closes.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Information-dense empty states: an empty deployments page shows 'git push origin main' in monospace (actionable and copyable) rather than a cartoon. Deployment status visible in browser tab favicon and page title — operators monitor without switching focus. Immediate 'deploying' state on trigger before server confirms (optimistic). Strategic 150–300ms delay before showing skeletons to prevent flicker. Skeleton states are layout-matched exactly to prevent layout shift on reveal. Geist typeface custom-designed for legibility at 12–14px with tabular numbers.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's authed route skeletons should be layout-matched to Home/Inbox/Results actual card geometry — not generic grey bars. The Vercel tab-favicon pattern is the extreme end of the 'status visible everywhere' principle; for Switchboard the minimum version is the Inbox 'needs you' count staying correct in real time. Tabular numerics for KPI numbers (fixing the data-tabular no-op) is a Geist-proven detail that makes money numbers feel precise.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: A finance-adjacent operator tool where every transaction is real money and the interface is built around that gravity. CMD+K command search makes finding any transaction, card, or setting faster than navigation menus. Real-time visibility into every transaction as it happens. Machine-learning auto-categorization surfaces as immediate feedback — the operator sees the system working on their behalf. Clean, fast, high information density without noise. Trust built through: knowing what you spent, on what, and whether it was correctly categorized — all visible before the day ends.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's 'Riley is spending your money on ads' is structurally analogous to Ramp's 'your card was just charged'. The model is: show the operator the transaction immediately, explain what category it fell into, and give them one-tap override. This is the Results page's design challenge — making the Riley spend legible at a glance, with a worsening ROI delta that reads as 'cool' concern rather than invisible gray ink.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Business banking built on the premise that a clean dashboard builds visual trust, and that the tone of voice carries as much trust as the visual design. Morphing animation for card state transitions (educates while delighting). Human, transparent, calm microcopy: 'We'll never access your funds without your permission' appears at the moment the user needs that reassurance. Consistency as confidence: same patterns across every screen signal that the product is carefully made. Premium through restraint: white space and clear typography over data density.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's editorial voice — warm, honest, never-fabricating — is already the Mercury model and is the product's biggest trust asset. The gap is that Mercury's voice extends to its empty states, error states, and loading states; Switchboard's voice is concentrated in a few peak moments (the Home weeknote, Results pullquote) and absent in the functional states that operators actually see during their daily workflow.

antipatterns: Optimism theater: showing a loading spinner or card freeze while waiting for a server response on an action that succeeds 99% of the time. The spinner communicates 'I don't trust this will work' — the exact opposite of what a governed AI revenue tool needs to convey.

Toast-as-undo for sequential approvals: TOAST_LIMIT=1 means every undo except the last one is silently evicted. In a morning queue-clearing session, this leaves the operator with no safety net on everything but the final item. The undo becomes theater.

Stale counts after action: displaying a 'Needs You: 5' header after the operator has approved 3 items, for up to 60 seconds, trains the operator not to trust any number in the product. Counts and feeds must stay in sync via a coordinated invalidation, not separate polling intervals.

The em-dash panel: shipping a confirmation screen for a money-spending AI action with hardcoded '—' placeholders labeled 'preview not yet wired' is the single highest-trust-cost possible ship. Empty is better than false. Honest fallbacks ('Estimated cost unavailable for this action type') preserve trust; developer notes in production destroy it.

Parallel token systems: defining the same agent color 2–4 times in conflicting hex/HSL formats so that any WCAG fix to one definition leaves the other three out of sync. The hardcoded-hex T object is structurally un-fixable without rewriting it to var() references.

Shipped dark mode without a complete dark palette: auto-triggering a .dark class on first load for dark-OS phone users and rendering editorial serif text as black-on-dark is worse than no dark mode. A feature that breaks 70% of surfaces on the platform the audience uses most is a net negative trust signal.

The command widget that reframes the product: a globally-mounted 'Operator Chat — Type a command' widget in raw blue that appears on every screen converts a 'trustworthy colleague' aesthetic into 'developer console you must learn'. Brand identity must be consistent at every breakpoint and every screen.

Heroic empty states for placeholder modules: permanently-empty sections labeled 'work in progress' or 'No active handoffs' on the Home screen — the daily driver — signal an unfinished product to the exact operator who should be receiving their morning briefing.

Confirmation modals for reversible actions: using a modal interrupt for a low-risk recommendation approval adds friction that trains operators to dismiss without reading. Reserve modals for genuinely irreversible consequences (account changes, high-spend commitments); use optimistic-plus-undo for everything reversible.

Nav chrome that competes with content: a navigation model that changes shape 3x across breakpoints and includes items unreachable on mobile teaches operators different products at different screen sizes. The restrained 3-noun primary IA (Home/Inbox/Results) is correct; making it incoherent across breakpoints undoes the restraint.

Generic skeletons that rearrange on load: showing grey bars that do not match the actual card geometry, then shifting layout when real content arrives, trains the eye to distrust the loading state. The skeleton must be the shadow of the content, not a placeholder.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox card removal (onMutate optimistic flow)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: On approve or decline, remove the card from the DOM immediately via React Query onMutate optimistic update. Use a CSS exit animation of ≤150ms on transform+opacity only — no height/margin animation. Simultaneously invalidate decisions.feed(), recommendations.all(), and the Home Needs-You count in the same onMutate. If the mutation fails onError, re-insert the card with a brief 'Could not save — try again' inline state. The TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY should be set to 5000ms (5 seconds) for the undo toast, and TOAST_LIMIT raised to match queue depth or the undo surface rebuilt as a persistent bottom-band that queues individual countdown timers per approval.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Linear and Superhuman both prove that card-leaves-before-server is the correct model for high-frequency decision triage. The current 60s poll + no optimistic removal means operators cannot trust the Inbox count. The TOAST_LIMIT=1 eviction destroys the safety net for every approval but the last.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet — 'What this changes / Money at risk' panel
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Populate the panel with fields available NOW from the Decision/recommendation payload: action type (human-readable sentence, e.g. 'Send a follow-up SMS to Maya Chen'), estimated cost or spend delta if available from the payload (show '~$0.02 per SMS send' from agent metadata rather than '—'), affected customer name/count, agent confidence tier, and the undoable-until countdown timer. Use honest fallbacks only for genuinely unavailable fields: 'Spend impact: not applicable for comms-only actions'. Remove the developer comments ('preview not yet wired / wiring it up next week') from production markup — these should be code comments, not rendered text.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Stripe's confirmation standard and fintech UX best practice both converge: the pre-commit screen must show what changes and what is at risk in plain language. Four em-dashes on the most trust-critical surface in the product undermine the entire editorial investment. The minimum honest panel costs one sprint to wire from existing payload fields.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home, Inbox, Results — loading states
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a (auth)/loading.tsx route-level shell that renders layout-matched skeletons for all three daily surfaces. Each skeleton must match the actual card geometry: Home weeknote block (drop-cap + 3-line text block + agent signature line), Inbox decision cards (agent avatar circle + 2-line title + risk chip + action buttons), Results KPI tiles (56px numeral + label + delta chip). Apply a 150ms show-delay via CSS animation-delay to prevent flicker on fast responses. Replace the bare 'Loading…' div in inbox-screen.tsx with the layout-matched skeleton. Fix the isLoading gate to the keys-pending-safe !data && !error pattern already used in mira-desk-page.tsx.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Vercel's design guidelines require skeletons to be 'stable' — matching final content exactly to avoid layout shift. The authed daily surfaces have zero loading states today while the marketing page has a polished skeleton. This is backwards: perceived performance investment should be highest where operators visit most.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: alreadyHandled (stale-409) reconciliation path
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Wire the already-built alreadyHandled flag in inbox-screen.tsx. On a 409 stale response, display an inline card state (not a toast) reading: '[Agent name] already handled by [teammate name] · [relative time]' with the card shifted to a muted/resolved visual state before auto-removing after 2s. This is a solved UX pattern (Slack's 'already reacted' inline state is the model) and the data is available from the 409 response body.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Silently closing a card that a teammate already handled gives zero information and trains operators to mistrust the queue. The existing infrastructure (alreadyHandled flag) was built for this exact case — it just needs to be passed down and rendered.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Design token system (globals.css + cockpit/tokens.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Migrate cockpit/tokens.ts in a single focused PR: replace each of the 18 hardcoded hex/rgba values with hsl(var(--agent-x)) references following the pattern already established in mira-config.ts. Extend tokens.test.ts with a drift guard that asserts every value in the T object resolves to a var() reference (no hex literals). Scope the migration to the live footprint only — verify ApprovalCard and roi-bar are dead code before migrating them. After the T migration, consolidate the v6.coral/agent-amber duplicates in tailwind.config.ts to reference the same globals.css custom properties.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Any WCAG color fix, dark-mode token, or brand update currently requires 3+ changes to stay in sync. mira-config.ts proves the migration pattern is already understood. The token unification is the keystone that unblocks dark mode completion, WCAG amber fix, and all agent identity consistency fixes downstream.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Dark mode toggle (Settings → Account)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Disable the Dark and System options in the theme selector in Settings → Account at the next release. Show only Light (with a tooltip: 'Dark mode coming soon'). Remove the prefers-color-scheme listener in use-theme.ts for the System case to prevent auto-trigger on dark-OS devices. Document the ~105 missing dark token definitions as a follow-up sprint gated on the token consolidation PR above. This is a one-hour change that eliminates a class of first-impression failures for mobile operators.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A shipped dark mode that breaks editorial tokens, agent identity, Mercury, and the entire hardcoded cockpit on first load for dark-OS phone users is a net negative vs. no dark mode. Vercel's dark mode works because it was architected from the start as a coherent system. Switchboard's was layered on. Ship clean.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox — keyboard triage model
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Implement J/K for next/previous card navigation (role=listbox on the feed, role=option on each card), E or Enter to approve the focused card, R to open the detail sheet (request review / see more), Esc to dismiss/defer. Surface keyboard hints inline on the focused card (faint 'E to approve / R to review' at bottom of focused card, only). Add Cmd+K command palette backed by cached decisions.feed() data for search and bulk-triage. The desktop two-pane layout already exists — wire the right pane to display on keyboard focus, not only on mouse click.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Superhuman's core insight is that keyboard triage compounds across 100 daily decisions. Switchboard's Inbox is structurally identical to Superhuman's email queue: a sorted feed of items demanding a binary or nuanced response. The pointer-only model is also a hard WCAG 2.1.1 keyboard accessibility failure on the product's core flow.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home — empty state on calm days
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Implement two distinct Home empty states: (1) First-run/pre-data: 'Alex is watching for new enquiries on [channel]. On a busy day, you'll see their recommendations here.' with a 'Send Alex a test message' CTA that deep-links to the channel. (2) All-clear/earned: Replace 'No active handoffs' hardcoded module with a conditional that only renders when workInProgressItems is non-empty. On calm-all-clear, render the Alex weeknote as the full Home surface (it already exists for this case) with a 'since you hired Alex' cumulative hero pulled from the AgentPanel data layer that already has this number.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The Eleken empty-state principles and Linear's minimal-monochrome standard both confirm: 'No active handoffs' on a hardcoded empty array reads as broken product, not calm success. The 'since you hired' cumulative framing already exists in the AgentPanel — bringing it to Home converts 'nothing happened today' into 'Alex handled 342 enquiries while you weren't looking'.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Operator Chat widget (auth layout)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Remove the bg-blue-600 globally-mounted 'Operator Chat — Type a command' widget from (auth)/layout.tsx, or replace it with an amber-styled, persona-consistent interaction surface that uses the operator-amber token, speaks in the product's editorial voice ('Ask Alex anything'), and is positioned as a collapsed icon rather than a persistent expanded widget. If the widget is a prototype/debug artifact, gate it behind an OPERATOR_CHAT_ENABLED environment flag so it does not appear in production or preview deployments.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A blue developer-console widget mounted on every authed screen contradicts the 'trustworthy colleague' aesthetic on every page an operator opens. Brand identity requires consistency at every breakpoint. If the feature is not launch-ready, it should not be visible to operators.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Results — worsening delta contrast
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: The deltaNeg class (currently ink-3 ~3.6:1 contrast) must meet WCAG AA at 4.5:1 for normal text. Darken to approximately ink-2 or use the operator-amber hue shifted toward cooler/lower lightness for worsening deltas — amber-adjacent 'caution' (not alarm-red, not invisible gray). Simultaneously fix the data-tabular no-op: add font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums to the KPI number CSS rule so KPI numerals stop jittering in proportional Inter. These are two CSS lines that make the most money-adjacent numbers in the product both legible and precise.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Results is where operators judge whether the agents are earning their keep. A worsening ROI delta that renders identically to a flat one (because deltaNeg is the lowest-contrast element on the page) means bad news is structurally invisible. This directly contradicts the product's 'honest, never-fabricating' voice. The Vercel Geist guideline — tabular numbers for comparisons — is the industry standard for financial dashboards.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding wizard — 'Go live anyway' gate
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace 'Go live anyway' (available at testedCount===0) with a softer but real gate: require at least one simulated scenario pass OR a 'Send Alex a test message from your own number' step that creates a real-channel proof. Wire useFirstRun (currently zero non-test consumers) to display a post-launch checklist on the first Home visit: 'Alex is live on WhatsApp · Here's what happens when a lead writes in [3-step visual]'. Reskin wizard CTAs to operator-amber (--sw-accent) to match the live product's action color.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Superhuman's onboarding insight — drill muscle memory before the operator touches real state — applies here: launching an AI customer-messaging agent with zero test runs is an unsafe default. The useFirstRun infrastructure was built for exactly this post-launch activation moment and sits as dead code. Wiring it costs one sprint and converts the 'empty Home after launch' failure state into a first-value delivery.

angle: Calm-tech and trust design for high-stakes money tools. The central thesis: a product that spends the user's money on their behalf must earn psychological permission for each action by making the stakes legible, the agent visibly present and working, the commit moment explicitly safe, and the recovery path always available. Calm tech (Amber Case / Xerox PARC) frames this as working with human attention rather than against it; the best fintech (Monzo, Wise, Ramp, Stripe) translates this into concrete interaction contracts at the three moments that matter most — before the commit, during the commit, and after the commit.

principles.principle: Friction proportional to irreversibility, not to importance
principles.why: Monzo's 'positive friction' doctrine distinguishes good slowdowns (borrowing, large transfers, irreversible actions) from bad ones (checking a balance, confirming a routine low-risk action). The friction budget is spent on stakes, not on frequency. In an AI-supervised spend product, this maps directly: a swipe-approve on a low-risk internal recommendation deserves zero friction; a first-time ad spend with a new audience deserves a named confirmation step with a real $ figure and a countdown undo window. Applying uniform friction everywhere destroys the operator's triage velocity and trains them to dismiss confirmations reflexively, which is the opposite of safety.
principles.source: Monzo positive friction doctrine (econsultancy.com/prudent-ux-for-banking-monzo-designs-positive-friction); Monzo magic moments blog (monzo.com/blog/2022/11/04/how-we-design-magic-moments-at-monzo)

principles.principle: Act first, undo second — never confirm-then-act for reversible decisions
principles.why: Superhuman's canonical optimistic UI insight: 'Actions complete visually before server confirmation; undo is the safety net, not loading spinners.' Confirmation dialogs before reversible actions feel bureaucratic and erode velocity without actually improving safety. The correct model is: assume success, remove the item immediately from the queue (FLIP animation), surface a branded undo toast with a real countdown bound to the actual undoable window. This is already the gold standard in email triage (Gmail, Superhuman) and needs to be the standard in an approval queue where operators process 5-15 items per session. The critical constraint: the undo must be guaranteed — a hidden, easily evicted, 16-minute-expiry toast is not an undo; it is theater.
principles.source: Superhuman design guide (blakecrosley.com/guides/design/superhuman); Gmail undo-send pattern; LogRocket optimistic UI (blog.logrocket.com/understanding-optimistic-ui)

principles.principle: The commit screen must show what actually changes, not a summary of what was requested
principles.why: Wise's core trust mechanism is showing the combined total, the exchange rate used, the fee, and the arrival estimate — not just 'Confirm transfer.' The user never has to trust the system's arithmetic; they verify it. For AI-driven spend actions, the equivalent is showing: what the agent intends to do (concrete action + parameters, not an abstraction), why it chose this (confidence + explicit uncertainty), what money is at risk ($ figure, not a dash), and what the reversibility window is. The HITL design pattern literature (DEV.to) confirms: requiring the reviewer to re-derive the decision from external systems is an anti-pattern that leads to rubber-stamp approvals, which destroys the safety guarantee the UI is claiming to provide.
principles.source: Wise UX teardown (userbrain.com); fee transparency study (lyssna.com/blog/fee-transparency-ux-wise-vs-revolut); HITL patterns (dev.to/omnithium/human-in-the-loop-patterns)

principles.principle: Calm technology operates at the periphery — only escalate to center of attention when action is required
principles.why: Amber Case's Principle 3 (calmtech.com): 'Technology should move easily from the periphery of our attention, to the center, and back.' A product that surfaces everything at maximum urgency trains users to ignore everything. The correct posture for a supervised-AI money tool: agents work in the background and appear in the user's peripheral awareness (a calm 'Riley is running' status, an agent aura that breathes rather than pulses) until they need a decision, at which point the notification is a precise, contextual call-to-action rather than an alert count. The 'Needs You' feed on Home is correct in principle; it fails in practice only because the count goes stale and items reappear after approval.
principles.source: calmtech.com Principle 3; Amber Case book (O'Reilly 2015); Calm Tech Institute principles (calmtech.institute/calm-tech-principles)

principles.principle: Design system coherence IS trust — visual inconsistency triggers subconscious alarm bells
principles.why: KOTA's analysis of Monzo/Revolut trust design identifies this explicitly: 'disjointed design feels risky' because 'minor visual inconsistencies can cause subconscious alarm bells to ring' in financial contexts. When an operator sees the same agent wearing a different color and face depending on which screen they're on, their nervous system registers inconsistency as unreliability, regardless of any rational evaluation. This is not an aesthetic preference; it is a trust mechanism. A single coherent token system is a prerequisite for trust, not a cosmetic improvement.
principles.source: KOTA fintech trust analysis (kota.co.uk/blog/how-fintech-brands-like-revolut-and-monzo-use-ux-to-build-trust); Phenomenon Studio fintech trust patterns (phenomenonstudio.com/article/fintech-ux-design-patterns-that-build-trust-and-credibility)

principles.principle: Skeleton screens for data-loaded views; the absence of a skeleton is a trust signal that the app is broken
principles.why: NN/G's skeleton screen research confirms: 'skeleton states improve perceived performance as the app appears to load faster than it actually does' and screens that open to blank space — or worse, to an immediate empty-state like 'All caught up' with pending items — feel broken or untrustworthy. In a high-stakes context, a blank screen or a false 'nothing needs you' state is not just a usability issue; it actively erodes the operator's confidence that the product is working on their behalf. Layout-matched skeletons, not spinners, are the correct primitive for route-level loads of the Home, Inbox, and Results surfaces that operators open every day.
principles.source: NN/G skeleton screens (nngroup.com/articles/skeleton-screens); Onething Design skeleton vs spinner (onething.design/post/skeleton-screens-vs-loading-spinners)

principles.principle: Agent identity creates presence, not decoration — a visibly 'working' agent is a trust signal
principles.why: Research on AI agent personality design (Frontiers in Computer Science, 2025) shows that 'anthropomorphic features — including dynamic state changes — create a sense of connection and attentiveness' and correlate with elevated trust. The key discipline is context-adaptive state (idle/working/halted) that is visibly derived from real system state, not hardcoded. This is distinct from gratuitous animation: the working aura of an agent that is genuinely running jobs communicates 'your money is being managed right now' in a way that flat letter-discs simply cannot. The Calm Tech constraint is that this signal must operate at the periphery — a subtle breathing state, not a pulsing alert.
principles.source: Frontiers in Computer Science 2025 (frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1531976); Agent personality design (pixelmojo.io/blogs/agent-personality-voice-design); calm tech periphery principle

principles.principle: Never collapse error into empty — honest negative states are the product's integrity signal
principles.why: The NN/G and HITL design literature both flag the same failure mode: when a system hides bad outcomes behind ambiguous states ('all caught up' when items are pending, a dash where a dollar figure should be), it trains users to distrust every positive signal. Monzo's plain-English doctrine ('never use industry speak or tech waffle') and Stripe's transparency approach both center on the same insight: a user who receives honest negative information ('Alex flagged this lead as low-confidence because the contact has no booking history') trusts the system more, not less, because they know it is not papering over problems. The corollary: a 'Money at risk: —' dash is not neutral; it actively communicates 'we are hiding something from you.'
principles.source: Monzo plain language doctrine (kota.co.uk); Stripe transparency (medium.com/design-bootcamp); HITL approval information architecture (dev.to/omnithium)

principles.principle: Progressive autonomy calibration: let the approval history earn the agent its trust level
principles.why: HITL design literature and agentic UX research converge on a single principle: 'progressive delegation directly addresses trust resistance by letting the user's own approval history set the pace of autonomy expansion.' An operator who has approved 50 low-risk recommendations and seen them all succeed has different evidence than one who just activated the agent. The autonomy thresholds (swipe-approve vs detail-confirm vs explicit spend-confirm) should visibly reflect this evidence, and the UI should make the current trust level legible — not because operators will tune it manually, but because seeing their own track record is itself a trust signal.
principles.source: Human-in-the-loop AI patterns (cognitivetoday.com/2026/05/human-in-the-loop-ai-approval-gate); agentic design patterns (agentic-design.ai/patterns); fintech UX 2026 (wsa.design/news/top-10-fintech-ux-design-best-practices-for-2026)

principles.principle: Graceful failure is a feature, not a fallback — design the error path as a trust-building moment
principles.why: Calm Tech Principle 6 (calmtech.com): 'Think about what happens if your technology fails — systems should default to usable states rather than complete breakdowns.' In a financial product, the failure path is the highest-stakes trust moment: a network timeout during an approval, a duplicate action by a teammate, a policy rejection. Monzo buffers payments for a few seconds specifically to allow 'undoing' them — the buffer is not a technical constraint but a deliberate trust investment. The 'alreadyHandled' stale-409 path is correct architecture precisely because it surfaces 'already handled by [teammate] 4m ago' rather than silently closing the card.
principles.source: calmtech.com Principle 6; Monzo payment-buffer pattern; Phenomenon Studio error microcopy (phenomenonstudio.com/article/fintech-ux-design-patterns)

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Positive friction at the right moments: Monzo deliberately slows down borrowing flows and large payment confirmations ('good friction') while keeping routine actions (balance check, notification review) instant. The card-freeze interaction uses a frost animation so the cognitive state of 'locked' is visually literal, not just textual. Payments are buffered for a brief window to allow undo before they clear. Magic moments — designed surprise and delight at key milestones — layer on top of a foundation of ruthless plain-English clarity. The principle: 'Most interactions should go unnoticed; magic should emerge from flawless execution, not novelty.' Net Promoter Score of 55 vs UK banking average of 16 is the measured result.
exemplars.takeaway: Calibrate friction to stakes, not to frequency. The undo buffer before a payment clears — not a confirmation dialog before the action — is the canonical pattern for reversibility in a money product. Apply to Switchboard's approval flow: the swipe commit should clear the card immediately (optimistic), with a branded countdown-undo anchored to the actual undoableUntil window, not a generic toast.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Fee-first transparency: Wise shows the combined total, fee, exchange rate, and delivery estimate before the user reaches the final confirm button — not in fine print after. Users describe it as 'everything appeared to be labeled on the page' and a Lyssna study showed 70% of users correctly identified total costs on first attempt (vs much lower for competitors). The confirmation screen names the specific state change ('£247.50 will leave your account, €285.03 arrives by Tuesday 4pm') rather than summarizing the request ('Confirm transfer'). Post-transaction, the status screen shows the full process log so users can verify what happened.
exemplars.takeaway: The 'What this changes' panel in Switchboard's approval detail is already the right idea — the problem is it ships with dashes instead of real data. The Wise model is the target: name the specific before-state, the specific after-state, the specific dollar amount committed, and the delivery/execution timeline. If a field cannot be populated from the existing Decision payload, omit it rather than filling it with a placeholder that signals 'we are hiding something.'

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Optimistic removal with FLIP animation: pressing E to archive removes the email immediately (150ms slide-right animation), focus moves to the next item, and server sync happens invisibly. Undo is surfaced as a toast with a visible countdown and keyboard shortcut (Z), serving as the safety net rather than a confirmation gate. The interface processes 50+ items in a session with zero loading spinners. J/K navigation creates a flow-state triage rhythm where decisions happen faster than conscious deliberation. Every keystroke receives instant visual closure — 'did that work?' anxiety is structurally eliminated by the architecture.
exemplars.takeaway: The Inbox approval queue in Switchboard is architecturally identical to an email triage inbox: a list of actionable items that should disappear on commit and be recoverable within a short window. Superhuman's pattern maps directly: onMutate optimistic removal, FLIP animation for remaining items, branded undo toast with real countdown, decisions.feed() invalidation immediately. The card must leave the screen the instant the operator commits — not after the network round-trip.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Policy-as-UX: Ramp's approval workflow surfaces the policy rule that triggered the review ('out-of-policy: exceeds $500 category limit for this employee') rather than just presenting a transaction for approval. The approver sees context (what the policy says, why this item was routed to them, who else is in the chain) inline in the approval card, without needing to navigate to a separate policy screen. Conditional threshold automation (auto-approve below $X, require review $X-$Y, escalate above $Z) makes the trust gradient legible and auditable — operators can see exactly which threshold their agent is operating under.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's approval card should surface the governance reason — 'Alex flagged this for your review because the estimated spend exceeds your $200 auto-approve threshold' — not just the action. This converts the operator from a rubber-stamp into an informed supervisor, which is the entire safety promise of the product.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Speed as trust signal: Linear saves views optimistically, keyboard shortcuts are the primary interaction model, and the triage inbox uses the same queue-processing metaphor as Superhuman. The Cmd+K command palette allows any action without navigation. Every action is available through multiple modalities (button, keyboard, context menu, search) so the operator can always find the canonical path. The result is that processing a queue feels like work being done, not work being navigated.
exemplars.takeaway: The j/k/a/d keyboard triage model — specifically the Inbox two-pane desktop view — should have a keyboard approval contract: j/k to move, a to approve, e to decline, Enter to open detail, Esc to close. This is table-stakes for power operators on desktop, and it is the pattern the current pointer-only decision-card architecture structurally lacks.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Calm through restraint: two Apple Design Awards for a product that explicitly strips out features rather than adding them. 'The app brings a sense of calm to your busy schedule through its clean interface — it strips out all the distractions and the fussiness, leaving pure, easy-to-access essentials.' Information density is precisely calibrated: Today View shows only what matters now, Upcoming allows at-a-glance load assessment. The design works by what it excludes. No status-broadcast widget, no command-line widget, no ambient analytics ticker — just the work.
exemplars.takeaway: The globally-mounted 'Operator Chat — Type a command' widget in bg-blue-600 is the direct anti-pattern of Things 3's calm. A calm tool for a time-poor operator does not surface a developer console on every screen. If operator chat has value, it belongs behind a focused entry point (a single FAB or a designated chat route), not as an ambient overlay that recasts the entire product as a command-line tool.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Transparency at the system level: public status page with real-time operational data, plain-language pricing (2.9% + 30¢ — no fine print), and open API documentation that demystifies how transactions work. The philosophy is that trust is built by making the system's behavior legible and predictable, not by hiding complexity behind a reassuring surface. Stripe's error messages are specific ('Your card's security code is incorrect') rather than generic ('Something went wrong'), so users can act on them.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's agent status and performance data should be visible, not withheld. An operator should be able to see 'Alex sent 14 messages today, 2 needed your review, 0 were declined' without navigating to a separate reports surface. System-level transparency — what the agent actually did, in plain language — is the Stripe-equivalent trust signal for an AI spend product.

antipatterns: Placeholder em-dashes in high-stakes panels: shipping 'Money at risk: —' or 'preview not yet wired' in a confirmation screen is worse than omitting the field entirely — it communicates 'we are hiding the answer from you' at the exact moment the operator needs to trust the product.

Undo-theater: a toast with a 16-minute expiry, TOAST_LIMIT=1 (evicted by the next action), and no countdown timer is not an undo. It is a visual that claims safety without providing it. The undo window must be bound to the real undoableUntil timestamp, must show a countdown, must pause on hover, and must not be evicted by subsequent toasts.

Stale queue: approving an item and having it silently reappear 60 seconds later (because the feed invalidation was missed) destroys the operator's confidence that their actions are having effect. This is the fintech equivalent of a payment that clears twice — the operator cannot tell if they are in control.

Uniform friction: applying the same confirmation overhead to a low-risk internal recommendation as to a first-time $500 ad spend trains operators to skip/dismiss confirmations reflexively. When every action feels equally weighty, the real high-stakes moments stop registering.

Command-line widget as ambient overlay: a 'Type a command' widget on every authed screen reframes a premium money product as a developer tool, and the raw blue-600 color signals 'debug console' rather than 'trustworthy colleague.' In a high-stakes trust context, unexpected UI patterns read as red flags.

Dark mode as shipped liability: a Light/Dark/System toggle that auto-activates on dark-OS phones and breaks 70% of surfaces (illegible black-on-dark Home, cockpits, Mira desk) is not a feature — it is a trust-eroding bug that can trigger with zero user action on first load.

False inbox-zero: the isLoading gate that can flash 'That's everything' with pending approvals on screen is the most dangerous antipattern in a supervised-AI product. An operator who is told there is nothing needing their attention while real decisions are pending will make decisions based on false information.

Four token systems for one brand: each agent appearing with 2-5 different colors and faces depending on which screen the operator is on registers as unreliability, not variety. In financial UX research, visual inconsistency triggers the same subconscious alarm as an unfamiliar bank logo on a payment screen.

Agents that never move: hardcoding avatar state to 'idle' means the operator never sees their agents working. The trust signal of a subtly breathing, visibly-active agent — 'Riley is currently analyzing your ad sets' — is dormant. Flat letter-discs convey no agency and no presence.

Onboarding that lets an untested AI go live with a soft click: 'Go live anyway' on zero passing tests is the agentic equivalent of a bank letting a customer authorize a wire transfer with a pop-up that says 'are you sure? (skip validation).' The activation gate IS the trust contract.

No route-level loading shells: opening Home, Inbox, or Results to blank space or immediate false-empty states signals that the product is broken. Layout-matched skeletons are the minimum viable trust signal for a premium authed surface.

Timeout-as-approval in AI agent flows: the HITL design literature is explicit — 'auto-approve when a review times out' is the canonical antipattern. Silence must map to denial or escalation, never to approval. This is the autonomy footgun that allows a patient agent to process actions without genuine human oversight.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox approval-commit moment (approval-detail-sheet.tsx + use-recommendation-action.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Implement true optimistic removal on commit: onMutate removes the card immediately with a FLIP animation that closes the gap, while invalidating both recommendations.all() and decisions.feed() simultaneously. The undo toast must be a branded component (not the default shadcn box) showing the action description, a visible countdown bar bound to the real undoableUntil timestamp, and a clear 'Undo' button — dismiss on countdown expiry, not on a 16-minute delay, and must not evict when a second action is committed (queue, don't replace).
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Superhuman's canonical pattern: the card leaving instantly is the confirmation that the action worked. A frozen card waiting for a network round-trip signals 'we don't know if that worked,' which is the opposite of calm confidence. The stale-queue problem (items reappearing at 60s) is the trust-destroying sequel: invalidating decisions.feed() on every commit closes this.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet — 'What this changes' panel (approval-detail-sheet.tsx:203-235)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace every em-dash placeholder with real derivable data from the existing Decision/recommendation payload: the specific action in plain English ('Send follow-up message to Sarah Chen about her Tuesday appointment'), the estimated dollar amount at risk (derive from recommendedBudget or estimatedCost fields; if genuinely unavailable, omit the field rather than showing '—'), the confidence level with an explicit uncertainty note if confidence < 0.7, and the reversibility window in human time ('Reversible for 2 hours · undo anytime before Alex sends'). The 'alreadyHandled' stale-409 path must surface 'Already handled by [teammate name] · 4 minutes ago' with a muted confirmation rather than a silent close.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Wise's trust model: the confirmation screen names the specific state change, not a summary of the request. An operator who can see exactly what will happen, how much it costs, and when they can reverse it is an informed supervisor. An operator looking at em-dashes is being asked to trust blindly — which is the opposite of the governed, you-approve product promise.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home, Inbox, Results — loading states (loading.tsx files, inbox-screen.tsx:181-182)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add route-level loading.tsx shells for all three authed daily surfaces with layout-matched skeletons: Home gets a skeleton for the verdict card, the needs-you count, and the work-in-progress module; Inbox gets a skeleton for 2-3 decision cards with the correct card proportions; Results gets a skeleton for the KPI tiles and the ROI chart area. Fix the Inbox isLoading gate to use !data && !error (the mira-desk-page.tsx:34 correct pattern) to eliminate the false 'That's everything' flash when keys are pending.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: NN/G: skeleton screens are perceived as shorter in duration than blank screens, and they help users build a mental model before content arrives. In a high-stakes product, a blank opening screen or a false empty-state is not neutral — it reads as 'something is broken.' The Home, Inbox, and Results surfaces are the three screens operators open every day; they deserve the same polish the marketing landing already has.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Agent avatar identity across Home, Inbox rows, AgentPanel header, cockpits
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Define one canonical avatar primitive per agent that expresses three states (idle / working / halted) derived from real agentStatus — specifically replacing the hardcoded state='idle' in inbox-agent-avatar.tsx:56. The working state should be a subtle breathing animation (the already-built OperatorCharacter aura is the right pattern) visible in the Home verdict header and the AgentPanel sheet header. Idle is the quiet default. Halted is visually distinct (desaturated, static). One face, one hue, everywhere — the T token system and globals.css must agree on Alex coral (resolve to one value) and Mira violet (resolve to one value) before this ships.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Frontiers in Computer Science 2025 research: context-adaptive agent state (not hardcoded idle) correlates with elevated trust and perceived anthropomorphic presence. Calm Tech constraint: the breathing aura operates at the periphery — it is visible but not demanding. The trust signal is 'Riley is working right now on your ad sets' expressed through gentle motion, not through a status badge. Inconsistent faces across screens register as unreliability at the subconscious level.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet — governance context panel
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a single-line 'Why this needs you' explanation to every approval card, surfacing the governance rule that triggered review ('Needs your approval · estimated spend $340 exceeds your $200 auto-approve threshold' or 'New audience — first-time targeting requires your sign-off'). This is derivable from the existing Decision.governanceReason or similar field; if not present, add it at the decision-creation layer.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Ramp's approval workflow pattern: the approver sees the policy reason, not just the transaction. An operator who understands WHY an item was escalated to them can make a calibrated decision; one who only sees the action must trust the system's judgment blindly. This is the difference between governed supervision and rubber-stamp approval.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: (auth)/layout.tsx — globally-mounted Operator Chat widget (operator-chat-widget.tsx)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Remove the globally-mounted 'Operator Chat — Type a command' widget from the authed layout shell and relocate it to a dedicated entry point (a single Cmd+K trigger or a focused chat route accessible from the sidebar) styled in the warm system (not bg-blue-600). If the widget is experimental or developer-facing, gate it behind an operator-level feature flag rather than shipping it to all users.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Things 3 / calm-tech principle: a calm tool for a time-poor, non-technical operator does not surface a developer console on every screen. The raw blue-600 color is nowhere in the warm token system and triggers the subconscious 'this looks like a different, unfamiliar product' alarm that erodes trust in financial UX contexts. The 'Type a command' framing reframes the product as a CLI tool, which directly contradicts the 'trustworthy colleague' positioning.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Settings → Account — Light/Dark/System toggle
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Hide or remove the Dark and System options from the toggle until a complete dark-mode token set exists across all surfaces (editorial tokens, agent identity tokens, shadow tokens, Mercury, and the cockpit token system resolved away from hardcoded hex). Ship a light-only mode explicitly. This prevents the current behavior where 'System' auto-activates on dark-OS phones and renders Home, Mira desk, and all cockpits illegible.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A shipped feature that breaks 70% of surfaces on first load with no user action is not a feature — it is a trust-destroying bug. Hiding the toggle is not a regression; it is correcting a gap between the feature's UI claim ('you can use dark mode') and its actual delivery. Ship dark mode completely or not at all.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home page — cumulative trust anchor and agent-status periphery
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a 'Since you hired Alex' cumulative hero to the Home page (the same pattern that lives in the AgentPanel sheet: 56px serif numeral, window-bound eyebrow label). Below the verdict card, show a periphery-style agent status strip — a single row per active agent, showing their identity hue, current state (idle/working), and one plain-English line of recent activity ('Riley reviewed 3 ad sets overnight'). This replaces the hardcoded empty 'No active handoffs' module with real, agent-sourced content.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Calm Tech Principle 3 (periphery): agents should be visible at the edges of awareness without demanding attention. The cumulative hero reframes the agent as an investment with a track record — the most powerful trust signal in a money product is 'look what your agent has done for you since you hired them.' The agent status strip is the ambient working-presence signal that communicates 'your team is on the job' on every Home load.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding activation — launch gate and post-launch landing
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Gate the 'Go live' action behind at least one passed simulated scenario (not just a soft 'Go live anyway' bypass), restyle the wizard CTAs to operator-amber (not --sw-text-primary near-black), and make the agent mark in the wizard render in its identity hue with the working aura. Post-launch, route to a 'You just launched' acknowledgment screen that wires the existing useFirstRun hook to show 'Alex is live on [channel]' with a 3-step 'what happens next' checklist — not the empty 'All caught up · 0 enquiries' Home.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The launch moment is the highest-stakes trust moment in the product lifecycle. An operator who launches a customer-messaging, money-spending AI agent with zero passed tests and lands on an empty, acknowledges-nothing Home has no evidence that the launch worked. The existing useFirstRun hook and the required data are already built — this is a wiring task, not a new build. The test gate signals 'we take your safety seriously'; the post-launch acknowledgment signals 'it worked.'

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Results — worsening ROI delta color and bad-news visibility
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: The deltaNeg color (currently ink-3 at ~3.6:1 contrast) must be visibly distinct from flat performance — use a muted amber-warm tone rather than alarming red, but ensure it reads as directionally different from neutral at a glance. The framing should follow Monzo's plain-language doctrine: 'Your cost-per-booking increased 18% this week · Riley is adjusting targeting' — naming the consequence and the agent response, not just showing a number in a color.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Honest negative states are the product's integrity signal. An operator who cannot tell at a glance whether their ad spend is working or leaking cannot supervise their agent. The color must pass AA contrast (at minimum 3:1 for large text, 4.5:1 for small text) and must be cognitively distinct from neutral — not alarmist (red implies crisis), not invisible (current ink-3 is nearly identical to flat). The Monzo model: plain English + action context, not just a metric color.

angle: AI agent / copilot product UX (2025-2026 state of the art): how leading products convey agent activity, autonomy levels, and human-in-the-loop control to earn trust for autonomous action — and how those patterns apply to Switchboard's specific verified gaps.

principles.principle: Autonomy is technical output; trustworthiness is design output. The interface determines whether earned capability gets deployed.
principles.why: Google Cloud's 2025 retrospective identified trust as the primary bottleneck for agent deployment — not capability. Sierra's architecture treats safety as a systems problem with bounded error rates, not a UI feature bolted on after. Products that shipped capable agents without transparent interfaces saw adoption collapse even when the agent performed correctly.
principles.source: Google Cloud 'AI Grew Up and Got a Job: Lessons from 2025 on Agents and Trust' (cloud.google.com/transform); Sierra 'From LLMs to Enterprise-Grade Agents' (sierra.ai/blog/enterprise-grade-agents)

principles.principle: Use an Autonomy Dial, not a toggle. Four stages — Observe & Suggest, Plan & Propose, Act with Confirmation, Act Autonomously — must be configurable per task type, defaulting to the most conservative level at onboarding.
principles.why: Smashing Magazine's 2026 agentic design patterns study found 'progressive delegation' (starting supervised, expanding as trust is earned) significantly outperformed full-autonomy-at-launch in adoption metrics. Monitor 'Setting Churn' — high churn signals trust volatility requiring model review, not UI redesign. Granular per-task controls matter: email scheduling vs. sending have different risk tolerances.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Designing for Agentic AI: Practical UX Patterns' (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02); Mantlr '10 AI Agent UX Patterns' (mantlr.com/blog)

principles.principle: Intent Preview is non-negotiable before any irreversible action. Show exact before/after outcomes in jargon-free language with three choices: Proceed / Edit / Handle Myself.
principles.why: The Smashing Magazine patterns study defines this as the 'pre-action consent contract.' Binary approve/cancel doesn't reflect real user comfort — the Edit path is what makes users feel collaborative rather than merely approving. Eleken's real-world survey of Privado Dining and Google Jules confirms: showing the sequential step-by-step plan (not just a summary) before execution is the single most trust-accelerating pattern in agentic systems.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Designing for Agentic AI' (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02); Eleken '6 Agentic UX Design Patterns' (eleken.co/blog-posts/agentic-ux-examples); Google Jules design (referenced in Eleken)

principles.principle: Optimistic removal + guaranteed undo beats 'Are you sure?' Flip the confirmation model: act immediately with a time-bounded branded undo toast, never a modal dialog before the action.
principles.why: Superhuman's 'E to archive' pattern — immediate UI update, background sync, undo via Z — eliminates a decision point while trusting the user. The 50-60ms internal latency target is the implementation of 'fast as thought.' The Smashing Magazine audit pattern specifies undo windows must be time-labeled ('Undo available for 15 minutes') and the <5% reversion rate threshold is a trust health metric: if users undo >5% of agent actions, the autonomy level is too high for that task type.
principles.source: Superhuman design analysis (blakecrosley.com/guides/design/superhuman); Smashing Magazine 'Action Audit & Undo' pattern (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02)

principles.principle: Separate the activity stream from the conversation thread. Agents need a dedicated 'what I'm doing' panel that exists independently of any chat UI.
principles.why: The Skins Factory analysis found that companies deploying agent functionality with copilot/chat UI patterns 'are going to fail' — the core mistake is conflating responsive (user-initiated) copilot patterns with autonomous (agent-initiated) monitoring needs. GitHub Copilot's unified sessions view, showing live progress and tool calls during execution, is the reference implementation: separate from chat, persistent, and filterable by verbosity.
principles.source: The Skins Factory 'AI Agent UX Design' (theskinsfactory.com/uiux-design-blog); GitHub Copilot Workspace sessions view (github.blog/changelog/2026-05-13)

principles.principle: Confidence signals must be binary for user decisions ('High confidence / Needs your review'), not percentage scores. Attach them to actions, not to the agent.
principles.why: Mantlr's testing found binary confidence labels outperform numeric percentages for user decision speed. The Smashing Magazine pattern specifies >0.8 Pearson correlation between model confidence and user acceptance as the success metric. Low-confidence outputs should pause automatically and route to the Explainable Rationale pattern. Attaching confidence to individual actions (not to 'the AI') prevents halo-effect over-trust.
principles.source: Mantlr '10 AI Agent UX Patterns' (mantlr.com/blog); Smashing Magazine 'Confidence Signal' pattern (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02)

principles.principle: Explainable Rationale should always use the 'Because you said X, I did Y' structure — linking to user-set rules, prior approvals, or context — never to model internals.
principles.why: The Smashing Magazine 'Explainable Rationale' pattern explicitly specifies this structure to prevent valid autonomous decisions from being read as bugs. Intercom Fin's implementation surfaces visible records of which guidance rules triggered each action in the Inbox — operators see their own rules reflected back at them, not opaque 'AI reasoning.' This is the pattern that converts a black-box into a legible colleague.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Explainable Rationale' pattern (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02); Eleken citing Intercom Fin design (eleken.co/blog-posts/agentic-ux-examples)

principles.principle: Graceful escalation requires a healthy escalation rate (5-15%), not minimization. Agents that escalate too rarely are over-confident; agents that never escalate signal over-automation.
principles.why: The Smashing Magazine patterns study defines 5-15% escalation frequency as the healthy range, with >90% recovery-success post-escalation as the complementary metric. Sierra's tiered supervisory model explicitly over-escalates on sensitive topics — 'much less autonomy...when someone is trying to exploit it than run-of-the-mill questions.' Decagon's approval gates for high-risk actions (large refunds, account deletions) are the enterprise implementation of this: not every action needs approval, but every high-stakes action does.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Escalation Pathway' pattern (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02); Sierra 'Enterprise-Grade Agents' (sierra.ai/blog/enterprise-grade-agents); Decagon 'Agentic Workflow' (decagon.ai/blog/agentic-workflow)

principles.principle: Agent personality must emerge from behavior, not decoration. Neutral, helpful tone; no sentient language ('I understand', 'I think'); trust through demonstrated reliability not witty banter.
principles.why: The letsgroto.com AI Copilot UX best practices analysis identifies 'over-humanization' — emotional phrases, sentient-sounding voice — as a top untrustworthy pattern. Operators in high-stakes domains (finance, healthcare) specifically respond negatively to anthropomorphic personality. Trust comes from: step-level intervention capability, audit trails without leaving primary screens, and explicit error recovery paths — not from the agent having a charming tone.
principles.source: letsgroto.com 'Mastering AI Copilot Design' (letsgroto.com/blog/mastering-ai-copilot-design); Smashing Magazine patterns study (smashingmagazine.com/2026/02)

principles.principle: An Action Receipt must follow every agent-executed action: what changed, where, which permissions were used, timestamp, responsible agent identity, and a rollback hook. This is not the undo toast — it is the persistent audit entry.
principles.why: The hatchworks.com 12-pattern framework identifies 'invisible actions' (user sees 'Done!' without evidence of what changed) as the primary chat-first failure mode. Google's 2025 agent retrospective listed atomicity and reversibility — agent undo stacks, transaction coordinators — as the top trust-builder in production systems. Salesforce Agentforce Observability's implementation (logging every agent input, reasoning step, action, and triggered guardrail in a unified dashboard) is the enterprise benchmark.
principles.source: Hatchworks 'Agent UX Patterns' (hatchworks.com/blog/ai-agents/agent-ux-patterns); Google Cloud 2025 agent retrospective; Eleken citing Salesforce Agentforce (eleken.co/blog-posts/agentic-ux-examples)

principles.principle: Design for the deployment-level trust arc, not the interaction. Shadow mode → supervised → semi-autonomous → autonomous is a product journey measured in weeks, not a settings screen.
principles.why: Google Cloud's 2025 autonomy spectrum (0% shadow mode → 75%+ autonomous) maps to concrete product stages: the agent observes and surfaces without acting, then acts with approval gates, then handles pre-approved task types autonomously. The swarmia.com 'Five Levels of AI Agent Autonomy' analysis confirms: higher autonomy hurts when tasks require judgment, review processes are bottlenecked, or the agent creates more review burden than it saves. Defaulting to conservative settings and expanding trust incrementally is the correct architecture.
principles.source: Google Cloud 2025 retrospective (cloud.google.com/transform); Swarmia 'Five Levels of AI Coding Agent Autonomy' (swarmia.com/blog/five-levels-ai-agent-autonomy)

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Optimistic removal with guaranteed undo: press E, the email immediately vanishes from the feed, focus moves to the next item, server sync happens in the background, a brief undo toast ('Email archived. Undo?') provides the safety net via Z. The 50-60ms internal latency target makes the interface feel 'fast as thought.' No modal confirmation dialog ever appears before a reversible action. The keyboard triage model (E/H/R/j/k) turns an approval queue into a zero-friction ritual.
exemplars.takeaway: The commit-moment UX on Switchboard's Inbox should feel like Superhuman's triage: card exits immediately on swipe-approve, the 'Needs You' count decrements instantly, a branded undo toast (not a shadcn default) with a live countdown tied to the real undoableUntil window appears at bottom. No freeze, no modal. The current TOAST_LIMIT=1 eviction and ~16-minute TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY are direct antipatterns of this model.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Keyboard-driven issue triage with FLIP list animation: items are removed from the queue with choreographed exit so the list never jumps or snaps. The AI auto-triage feature (launched September 2025) suggests priority and assignee before executing, and applies with one keystroke, but the user remains in the loop as the decision-maker. The product's restraint — no chat widget, no 'AI is thinking' anthropomorphism — signals that intelligence is infrastructure, not a persona.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Inbox card removal must be animated (Framer Motion AnimatePresence is already installed but has zero usages). The FLIP pattern eliminates the 'queue snaps closed' problem identified in the audit. Linear also demonstrates that agentic suggestions (triage, priority) can be surfaced without a chatbot widget — the agent's judgment appears in the data, not as a conversational interruption.

exemplars.product: GitHub Copilot Workspace / Copilot Coding Agent
exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: The Unified Sessions View (JetBrains, May 2026) shows live status for running and queued sessions with real-time tool calls visible during execution and a change summary at completion — all in a dedicated sidebar panel separated from chat. The plan-then-execute flow shows multi-step strategy before any code changes. Pause/resume works across days without losing context. Multiple agents appear as manageable objects in the sidebar, not as a monolithic 'AI'.
exemplars.takeaway: The AgentPanel in Switchboard should become the Unified Sessions View equivalent: a dedicated, persistent sheet (not a chat drawer) showing what Alex/Riley/Mira is actively working on right now, what it completed, and what's pending approval — derived from real agent status, not hardcoded idle. The 'agents as objects' mental model (each agent visible, pauseable, with scope boundaries) is the right architecture for Switchboard's three-agent system.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Agent Traces give step-by-step visibility into decision-making — not just what happened but why. The tiered supervisory model applies differential guardrails: maximum adherence on adversarial/illegal actions, medium tolerance on tone/phrasing. Supervisors can escalate, intercept, or steer. Post-turn and post-conversation reviews combine fast detection with high-precision reasoning. The December 2025 Gap.com jailbreak incident publicly demonstrated that misconfigured guardrails (not absent ones) caused the failure — a lesson that visible, auditable guardrail state matters.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's approval detail sheet needs to show which guardrail tier triggered each approval request — not just the action, but the governance reason ('This message goes to a real customer' / 'This commits $X in ad spend'). Sierra's model of bounded error rates (not perfection) is the right framing for the 'Money at risk' panel: show the real dollar estimate, the confidence level, and the specific irreversible consequence, rather than the current '—' placeholders.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Watchtower provides continuous monitoring that surfaces compliance risks, sentiment issues, and quality concerns, with every model call and decision traceable. Version control via Git-based tracking lets operators experiment in isolated workspaces without affecting live agents. A/B testing with built-in experimentation evaluates changes against control groups before full rollout, tracking CSAT and resolution rate impact. Human corrections are captured and fed back into agent improvement.
exemplars.takeaway: The 'work log' Switchboard already surfaces in the AgentPanel should function like Decagon's Watchtower output: not just a list of actions, but filterable by risk level, with confidence indicators and a visible record of which rules/governance gates fired. The current read-only panel is the right instinct; it needs live data from the actual agent execution path, not placeholder text.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Drag-and-drop conditional workflows with granular HITL checkpoints: each step can be set to 'run automatically' or 'pause for approval' at the operator's discretion. The agent flags items it considers 'too sensitive for autonomous handling' and routes them to a human decision queue without interrupting the rest of the workflow. The September 2025 Claude Sonnet 4.5 integration enables 30+ hour autonomous operation windows while preserving the approval architecture.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's per-agent autonomy controls (currently stored but not enforced, per the audit) should manifest as Lindy-style per-action-type approval gates: 'Alex can send follow-up messages automatically, but any message mentioning a price or offer must come to Inbox first.' This is the mechanical implementation of the Autonomy Dial principle at the Switchboard surface level.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Agents are first-class objects in a sidebar with their conversation, diffs, and plans visible simultaneously. Multiple agents run in parallel (refactoring, test-fixing, UI polish) and the user hops between them like switching terminals.
