applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: This is the product's strongest candidate for a signature moment in the Walter sense — repeatable, structurally significant, emotionally appropriate. The implementation decision: use the already-installed framer-motion (zero current usages) with AnimatePresence wrapping the card list and layout prop on each card. The card exit should be: opacity 0 + slight downward translate (20px) over 220ms ease-out — a card that settles rather than vanishes. Surrounding cards FLIP to their new positions automatically. The total duration of 'approval registered → queue settled' should be under 280ms. Critically: replace the EXIT_MS=280 CSS transition race by using framer's onExitComplete callback to trigger the next state. Under prefers-reduced-motion, fall back to immediate removal with no animation (the global guard already handles this). This converts the current 'card freezes → snaps closed' into a physical confirmation that the action was received.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Monzo frost-animation principle: the animation mirrors the conceptual action. A card leaving the queue after approval is conceptually a task being discharged — the animation should feel like completion. The Motion docs confirm layout animation is the correct primitive (FLIP, high performance via CSS transform). The framer-motion dependency cost is already paid; zero usages makes this a pure upside implementation.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval flow — confidence signal and 'already handled' state (use-recommendation-action.ts, dispatch-action.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Two specific emotional design additions at the commitment moment: (1) Wire the 'alreadyHandled' stale-409 path that is built but never passed — when an operator approves an item a teammate handled 4 minutes ago, the modal should show 'Already actioned by [teammate name] · 4 min ago. No action needed.' This is both honest (Rams) and emotionally safe (the operator is not blamed for a race condition). (2) Add a confidence chip to the approval card for low-certainty recommendations, following the Smashing Magazine Confidence Signal pattern: a muted yellow pill labelled 'Alex is less certain than usual — worth a closer look' rather than a binary high/low. This directly applies Walter's evolution beyond delight: 'when users are uncertain, lean into that emotion rather than hiding it.'
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Smashing Magazine: confidence signals build trust by demonstrating agent self-awareness. Walter (2nd ed.): design for the full emotional spectrum, including uncertainty. The 'alreadyHandled' fix is the highest-ROI copy investment in the product — it converts a silent failure into a moment of grace ('your teammate already handled this, you're not behind').

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Results — bad news legibility (results.module.css deltaNeg, deltaNeg color token)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: This is a delight-through-honesty surface. The emotional design principle: bad news delivered clearly and calmly is more trustworthy than bad news buried in low-contrast gray. The specific fix: raise deltaNeg from ink-3 (~3.6:1) to a dedicated --delta-negative token at approximately hsl(20 40% 40%) — a muted terra-cotta that reads as 'cooler' (not alarming red, not invisible gray, not the same as the primary operator-amber). This should also receive a distinct icon treatment: a small downward arrow at the same muted hue, so worsening ROI is glanceable rather than identical to flat. The emotional message: 'we show you the truth, including the uncomfortable parts, because you need the truth to make decisions.' This is the reflective-level trust signal for the Results surface.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Norman reflective level: the product communicates what it values. A product that buries bad news communicates it would rather you not know. Rams: good design is honest. Monzo's 'good friction' pattern applies here — bad news should be clear, not alarming. The medspa operator checking Results on Monday morning needs to be able to glance and say 'Riley's ads were down this week' — the current near-invisible deltaNeg prevents that.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding / activation (onboarding wizard, post-launch Home, useFirstRun hook)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: The onboarding payoff moment — 'Alex is now live on your WhatsApp' — is the product's first opportunity to fire the reflective level ('I made a good decision'). Currently the wizard uses system-font black CTAs and the launch dumps onto an empty Home. Three specific emotional design investments: (1) Re-skin the wizard CTAs to operator-amber with Alex's identity hue on the agent mark (breathing, not static) — this is the first time the operator sees their agent; it should feel alive. (2) Wire useFirstRun (currently dead code with zero non-test consumers) to the post-launch Home: show 'Alex is live on WhatsApp. Here is what happens next: [1] First inquiry arrives → Alex responds within 90s. [2] Anything needing your decision appears in Inbox. [3] Results track your bookings over time.' This is a narrative arc, not a feature tour. (3) Gate 'Go live anyway' behind at least one successful simulated scenario pass — or rename it 'Launch without a test run' so the operator is making an informed choice.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Walter's hierarchy: the pleasurable tier requires functional, reliable, and usable to already hold. Onboarding is the first functional test. The post-launch 'All caught up · 0 open enquiries' screen violates reliability (the product looks broken when it is actually working correctly — Alex just hasn't received an inquiry yet). The useFirstRun narrative converts an empty state from 'broken' to 'ready and waiting.'

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Token system / design system unification (cockpit/tokens.ts, globals.css, use-theme.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: This is the emotional design infrastructure item — not visible to operators but responsible for every visible inconsistency. The recommendation: (1) Disable the Dark/System toggle in Settings → Account until a complete .dark palette exists (covering editorial, agent, shadow, and cockpit tokens). A shipped toggle that breaks 70% of surfaces on first use is a visceral-level trust failure for any operator on a dark-OS phone. Hiding it is honest. (2) Migrate cockpit/tokens.ts 'T' hex values to hsl(var(--...)) references using the pattern that mira-config.ts already demonstrates — this makes every cockpit component themeable and removes the four-systems fragmentation. The migration can be incremental, starting with the live components only. (3) Add a drift-guard test in tokens.test.ts that asserts every agent identity value in globals.css, tailwind.config.ts, and any inline hex is consistent. This prevents the Alex coral having three different values across three files.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Norman visceral level: inconsistent colors across surfaces fire a low-level wrongness signal that operators cannot articulate but feel as 'this product isn't quite finished.' Mercury's emotional premium comes from meticulous calibration of a single design vocabulary. The four-system fragmentation is the structural root cause of most of Switchboard's emotional floor problems. The toggle fix is urgent (ships a breakage on dark phones today); the token migration is medium-term but is the keystone that enables everything else.

angle: Decision / approval / inbox-triage UX for time-poor, high-stakes operators supervising AI agents

principles.principle: Optimistic removal is the contract, not a shortcut. Act instantly on the UI; reconcile with the server in the background; make Undo the safety net, not a pre-flight 'Are you sure?' dialog.
principles.why: Superhuman targets 50ms response times for archive/approve because speed is the emotional experience. Confirmation dialogs interrupt flow and signal distrust; optimistic-then-undo trusts the user and still provides recovery. For approval queues, each extra round-trip interruption compounds into cumulative anxiety. Gmail's Undo Send — hold for 5 seconds, show a countdown, let the user cancel — is the canonical model: the action feels done immediately, safety is present but not intrusive.
principles.source: Superhuman design study (blakecrosley.com); Gmail Undo Send; Smashing Magazine — Designing for Agentic AI (2026)

principles.principle: Make the next action obvious and automatic. After every decision, the interface should surface the next item without forcing a navigation step back to a list.
principles.why: Superhuman's core flow insight: archiving an email in Gmail drops you back to the list — a decision about what to do next. In Superhuman, archiving surfaces the next email immediately, sustaining flow state. Csikszentmihalyi's flow requires 'always knowing what to do next.' Every return-to-list in an approval queue is a micro-interruption that fragments momentum and re-introduces decision fatigue.
principles.source: Rahul Vohra, 'Designing for Flow' (blog.superhuman.com); 7 Game Design Principles applied to email (blog.superhuman.com)

principles.principle: Friction must be proportional to stakes and inversely proportional to reversibility — not uniformly applied to everything.
principles.why: The classic three-factor matrix: reversibility, complexity, frequency. A low-risk routine item that can be undone warrants zero friction — swipe right, done. A money-spending or customer-facing action that is hard to reverse warrants intentional friction: a detail panel showing concrete impact, a named $ amount, a second deliberate tap. The same button label and same gesture for all items signals that the system cannot distinguish between them — which is the scariest possible message when an operator is trusting AI with real spend.
principles.source: Joel Pascual, 'A UX Guide to Destructive Actions' (medium.com/design-bootcamp); intentional-friction-in-ux-design (newsletter.designproject.io); NN/G indicators-validations-notifications

principles.principle: The confirmation detail must answer four honest questions: What will this do? What changes (before/after)? What money is at stake? Can I undo this?
principles.why: Wise, Monzo, and Revolut are the fintech gold standard: show the exact exchange rate, the fee, the recipient, and the ETA before the user commits. These four fields are the minimum that separate 'informed consent' from 'blind rubber-stamping.' When any field is 'preview not yet wired' (as in Switchboard's current approval-detail-sheet.tsx), the panel actively undermines trust — it signals the product itself doesn't know what the action will do.
principles.source: Wise transfer UX (wsa.design fintech best practices); Brex expense approval patterns; Smashing Magazine agentic AI patterns — 'Intent Preview'

principles.principle: Undo needs a visible countdown bound to a real, honest window — and it must queue, not evict on the next action.
principles.why: A toast that disappears after 16 minutes (Switchboard's TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY=1000000ms) is useless as a countdown signal; a toast that evicts on the next approval (TOAST_LIMIT=1) actively lies about safety. The correct model: a branded Undo component with a live shrinking progress bar bound to the real undoableUntil timestamp, persisting in a queue so a second approval does not silently destroy the first undo opportunity. The countdown paradox: too short (< 3s) creates panic; 5–10s is the sweet spot for most actions; longer windows should be rendered as text ('Can be undone until 2:47pm'), not as ticking bars.
principles.source: Gmail Undo Send (5s default, configurable to 30s); LinkedIn Toast UX guidelines (Vitaly Friedman, linkedin.com); Smashing Magazine agentic AI — Action Audit & Undo

principles.principle: The count of 'things needing you' must be a real-time mirror of the actual queue — stale counts are more damaging than no count.
principles.why: A badge that reads '5' after the operator has cleared 3 items is not merely wrong — it signals that the product's own attention signal cannot be trusted. The Zeigarnik Effect means unfinished-task signals (badges, counts) occupy cognitive space even subconsciously; a stale count keeps that cognitive load open after the work is done. The fix is not polling — it is onMutate optimistic-decrement that adjusts immediately on action, confirmed by server invalidation.
principles.source: NN/G indicators-validations-notifications; Zeigarnik Effect (Journal of Organizational Behavior 2024, cited via zeroinbox.ai); notification center best practices (Courier blog)

principles.principle: Concurrent handling must be surfaced gracefully, not silently closed. 'Already handled by [teammate] · 4m ago' is a trust signal; a silent close is a ghost.
principles.why: Collaborative approval queues create race conditions: a teammate approves while you are reading the detail panel. The right UX pattern is presence-and-prevention first (show who is viewing or has acted), then graceful notification second ('Alex already approved this · 4 min ago — here is what they chose'). A silent 409 close is the worst outcome: the operator wonders if their action registered, whether something was missed, and why the card disappeared. The pattern is established in collaborative tools from Notion CRDTs to Superhuman Team Reply Indicators — visibility prevents the collision before it happens.
principles.source: Fernando UX — Conflict Resolution in Collaboration (fernandoux.com); Superhuman Team Reply Indicators (help.superhuman.com); Smashing Magazine agentic AI escalation pathway

principles.principle: The emotional peak of triage is clearing the queue — design explicitly for the payoff, not just the items.
principles.why: Inbox Zero is a dopamine event: the brain releases reward on task completion (completion bias). Superhuman makes Inbox Zero a concrete, achievable goal and celebrates the moment. Things 3 delivers haptic feedback on task completion. The payoff state — 'You're all caught up' — is as important to design as the triage items themselves. An empty state that reads as 'broken' or 'asleep' wastes the reward moment that drives return behavior.
principles.source: Rahul Vohra 7 Game Design Principles (blog.superhuman.com); Things 3 haptic completion (tapsmart.com); alias.email Psychology of Inbox Zero; zeroinbox.ai — Zeigarnik Effect study

principles.principle: Skeleton screens should structurally match the content they represent and appear within one render frame of navigation — never show a blank screen, never show a false 'all clear'.
principles.why: NN/G research: skeleton screens reduce perceived wait for 2–10s loads by letting users build a mental model of the incoming content. The critical failure mode is the false-empty state: showing 'That's everything!' before data has loaded is not just incorrect, it actively destroys trust in the agent system. The !data && !error gate (not isLoading) is the technically correct React Query check for this because disabled/pending queries skip isLoading.
principles.source: NN/G Skeleton Screens 101 (nngroup.com); Next.js loading.tsx streaming docs; LogRocket skeleton loading UX

principles.principle: For high-stakes AI-supervised actions, surface a confidence signal alongside the decision — not as a probability percent, but as a plain-language qualifier.
principles.why: Automation bias (over-trusting AI) and under-trust (rejecting all recommendations) are both failure modes. Smashing Magazine's agentic AI research recommends confidence signals — but for a time-poor medspa operator, '92%' is not meaningful. Plain-language qualifiers like 'Alex is confident about this' vs. 'Alex flagged this for your review' communicate the same signal in the operator's register. The risk chip pattern Switchboard already uses ('low risk', 'financial', 'external') is the correct vocabulary — extend it to include an agent certainty tier.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine — Confidence Signal pattern (2026); Switchboard existing risk-chip pattern (audit, keepThese)

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Three interlocking patterns make triage feel like a game rather than work: (1) Optimistic instant removal — the item vanishes at 50ms, focus shifts to the next email automatically, Undo appears with 'Z' shortcut as a 5-second safety net. No confirmation dialog. (2) Keyboard-first flow — J/K navigation, E to archive, the command palette as a training wheel that makes itself obsolete. The design targets flow state by removing every 'what do I do now?' decision point. (3) Game design principles: Inbox Zero is a concrete achievable goal; the moment of clearing the queue is designed to feel like triumph. Rahul Vohra explicitly applies Csikszentmihalyi's flow — challenge matched to skill, immediate feedback, obvious next action.
exemplars.takeaway: The Switchboard Inbox should feel like triaging with a skilled colleague, not processing a form. Optimistic card removal (onMutate removes the card, focus moves to next item, 'Undo · 8s' appears in a countdown component), auto-advance to the next pending decision, and a crafted 'all clear' payoff state are the three mechanics that convert 'task' into 'satisfying'.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Linear's triage feature — accept to backlog, escalate to current cycle, decline with mandatory comment, snooze — is a four-verb decision grammar applied uniformly to every incoming issue. Keyboard shortcuts make each verb a single keystroke. The mandatory decline comment forces honest rationale rather than silent dismissal. Linked attachments preserve context without requiring duplication. The design principle is throughput through vocabulary reduction: every item gets exactly four possible outcomes, and you learn the grammar once.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's approval grammar should be explicit and small: Approve (green), Decline (with a mandatory one-line note), Defer (snooze to a time), and Escalate (flag for team). The verbs should be the same across every surface — Home, Inbox card, detail sheet — so operators develop muscle memory. Right now 'approve' lives in three different UI surfaces with different behaviors and no keyboard equivalent.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: HEY solves the cognitive architecture problem upstream of triage: by triaging senders (not messages) once into Imbox / Feed / Paper Trail, the daily decision queue contains only items the operator has already pre-categorized as important. The Screener is a one-time approval that amortizes future cognitive load. This is the correct mental model for AI oversight: the operator does not triage every individual message from Alex — they establish trust rules once and monitor exceptions. HEY also pioneered 'Screen in & Reply in one swoop' — collapsing two steps (approve sender, reply) into one gesture.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Inbox should distinguish between 'items requiring a decision today' (the true approval queue) and 'items providing context or receipts' (outcomes, audit trail). Mixing them forces the operator to re-triage on every load. The Home 'Needs You' hero already does this separation at the macro level — the Inbox should reflect the same hierarchy rather than showing a flat feed.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Wise's transfer confirmation screen is the gold standard for 'money at stake' panels: you see the exact amount you send, the exact amount the recipient receives, the exchange rate with a timestamp, the fee as a line item, and the estimated arrival time — all on one screen, before you tap Confirm. Nothing is 'preview not yet wired.' The design treats the confirmation as an informed consent moment, not a formality. Crucially, Wise shows the before state (your balance) and the after state (what they receive) so the operator can verify the real-world consequence of the action.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's 'What this changes' panel in the approval detail sheet should follow the Wise pattern exactly: show the before value, the after value, the $ amount at stake (from the real Decision payload's budget/spend fields), and the reversibility window. If a field cannot be computed yet, omit it rather than showing '—' with an excuse. An honest incomplete panel ('We don't yet know the exact spend impact') is more trustworthy than a dishonest placeholder.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Gmail's Undo Send (2009, now standard) proved that 'act immediately + grace period' is superior to 'confirm before acting' for most high-stakes actions. The UX: tap Send, the email is held for 5–30s (user-configurable), a banner with a visible countdown and Undo link appears. The action feels done (reducing the cognitive cost of committing), but recovery is trivially easy during the window. The critical design decision: the countdown is visible and shrinking — it communicates exactly how long the window is without requiring the user to guess.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Undo safety net should be a branded countdown component (not an un-timed shadcn toast) with a shrinking progress bar bound to the real undoableUntil timestamp from the backend. The component should persist in a FIFO queue of max 3 items so that approving two decisions in rapid succession still leaves both undoable. The component should auto-dismiss at expiry with a subtle 'committed' micro-animation — not just disappear.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Things 3 makes task completion physically satisfying: tapping the checkmark delivers haptic feedback (a light impact), the completed item briefly holds position showing it crossed off, then exits into the Logbook with a satisfying animation. The design respects both the completion (you see your work acknowledged) and the momentum (it exits cleanly without cluttering the remaining queue). The progress arc showing 'X completed today' provides the accumulation signal that makes clearing the queue feel like an investment, not a treadmill.
exemplars.takeaway: When a Switchboard approval card is committed, the interaction should feel resolved: a success haptic (UINotificationFeedbackGenerator .success on iOS web), the card should animate out with intention (not snap or teleport), and the queue count should decrement immediately. The Undo component appearing is not a failure signal — it's the confidence signal that the action was registered and the operator is protected.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Brex's mobile approval flow surfaces three pieces of context before a manager taps Approve: the merchant name + amount, whether it is above a pre-set threshold (with a visual flag), and the current department budget consumption. Exceptions are visually distinguished from routine items at the list level — managers scan for the flagged items rather than reading every line. This is the 'exceptions clearly flagged' pattern: the approval queue is not a uniform list of equal-weight decisions; it is a risk-sorted triage surface where the operator's eye goes directly to the items that need thought.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Inbox card should front-load the risk tier visually (the existing risk chip is correct but should be the first element in the card hierarchy, not buried). High-stakes items (financial, external, irreversible) should be visually differentiated at the list level — a heavier left-border accent in the agent's identity hue, for example — so the operator's eye goes to them first without reading every card.

antipatterns: Confirmation dialogs before routine, reversible actions — 'Are you sure you want to approve this?' for a low-risk item destroys flow and signals distrust. Undo-after is always superior for reversible actions.

Placeholder copy in the confirmation detail panel ('preview not yet wired', 'Money at risk: —'). A blank or honest incomplete panel is better than a labeled placeholder — it teaches operators to ignore the most important information surface.

TOAST_LIMIT=1: an Undo queue that evicts on the next approval silently removes the safety net without notifying the operator. The operator who approved two items in quick succession has no undo on the first.

TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY as a de facto timer — 16-minute timeouts mean the Undo toast is not a countdown, it is a floating element that never conveys the actual undo window. Operators cannot trust or act on information they cannot read.

Stale 'Needs You' counts that do not update optimistically on approval. A count that reads '5' after the operator cleared 3 items is more damaging than no count — it teaches the operator to distrust the product's own attention signals.

Silent 409 / alreadyHandled close: when a teammate has already handled an item, closing the panel silently leaves the operator uncertain whether their action registered or whether something was missed. Always surface who acted and when.

Uniform card weight in the triage list — presenting a 'message a customer' item and a '$500 ad spend' item with identical visual weight forces the operator to read every card before knowing which decisions need thought.

Return-to-list after each decision — after approving a card, dropping the operator back to the full list with no auto-advance interrupts flow state and introduces a 'what do I do next?' decision that should not exist.

False 'all clear' empty state before data has loaded — showing 'That's everything!' or 'All caught up' before the API response arrives (the isLoading vs !data && !error gate failure) is the most trust-destroying possible moment: the operator believes they are done and walks away.

Generic blue command-line widgets overlaid on every approval screen — framing a trust-critical approval flow as a 'developer console' contradicts the product's colleague metaphor and increases cognitive load for non-technical operators.

Dark mode that auto-activates on a dark-OS phone without the operator choosing it, breaking 70% of surfaces — in the approval and money-confirm flows especially, illegible rendering (black ink on dark) is not a cosmetic issue, it is a safety failure.
Batching undo with modal confirmation for the batch — 'Approve all 5 items? Are you sure?' is the worst of both worlds: it introduces pre-flight friction AND removes per-item undo granularity.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox card approval (inbox-screen.tsx + use-recommendation-action.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Implement full optimistic-removal on commit: onMutate removes the card from the local list immediately (no freeze-on-network), auto-advances focus to the next pending item (or transitions to the 'all clear' state), and invalidates both recommendations.all AND decisions.feed() so the header count decrements in the same render cycle. The card should animate out using the already-installed framer-motion AnimatePresence — not snap or teleport. Wire the existing data-exiting attribute and fix the EXIT_MS/CSS transition race.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The current behavior — card freezes during network round-trip, feed never refreshes, 'Needs You' count stays stale for up to 60s — is the most-damaging trust failure in the product. Operators who clear 3 items and still see '5 pending' learn to distrust the product's own attention signals. This is directly analogous to Gmail vs. Superhuman's core differentiator.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet (approval-detail-sheet.tsx) — 'What this changes' panel
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace the four em-dash placeholders with an honest panel derived from the real Decision/recommendation payload. Pattern: Before (current state) → After (post-approval state) → $ at stake (from budget/spend fields if present) → Undo window (human-readable: 'Can be undone for 8 minutes'). If a field is genuinely not computable from the current payload, omit the row entirely — do not show '—' with a label. Follow Wise's transfer confirmation model: the operator should be able to glance at this panel and say 'yes, I understand what this commits.' Ship the producer population (the backend fields that feed this panel) in the same PR as the UI — per the safety-gate-needs-producer-population lesson.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The current 'preview not yet wired / wiring it up next week' copy in the most trust-critical moment of the entire product is the single highest-severity gap in the audit. Operators are trusting AI to spend their money and message their customers. An honest incomplete panel ('We cannot yet estimate the exact spend impact') is dramatically more trustworthy than a placeholder with an apology.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Undo component (use-toast.ts / toast infrastructure)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace the un-timed shadcn toast with a branded countdown Undo component: (1) A shrinking progress bar visualizing the real undoableUntil window from the backend payload. (2) TOAST_LIMIT raised to 3 (FIFO queue, not eviction) so two rapid approvals both remain undoable. (3) TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY replaced by dynamic duration computed from undoableUntil - Date.now(). (4) The component uses the existing .toast-undo CSS class (already in the codebase) for warm-system styling — amber border, no blue. (5) On expiry, the component exits with a 'committed' micro-animation (opacity fade + subtle checkmark) rather than vanishing. The countdown should pause on hover/focus per accessibility guidelines.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The current 16-minute un-dismissing toast and TOAST_LIMIT=1 eviction are both safety theater — they look like an undo mechanism but do not function as one. An undo component that communicates exactly how long recovery is available, and that survives a second approval, turns Undo from an afterthought into the confidence signal it should be.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: alreadyHandled stale-409 path (inbox-screen.tsx, dispatch-action.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Wire the already-built alreadyHandled boolean in the mutation response to display an inline inline-context notification (not a toast) within the card's exit position: 'Already [approved/declined] by [teammate name] · [relative time]'. The card should then animate out normally. This follows the conflict-prevention-before-conflict-resolution principle: if Superhuman-style 'who is viewing this' presence indicators are added later, this graceful notification is the fallback for when prevention fails.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A silent close on a 409 leaves the operator uncertain whether their action registered. Surfacing the teammate's action converts a confusing failure into a trust signal: the system is aware of the concurrency, it protected the operator from a double-action, and it tells them what happened.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox card visual hierarchy (inbox-decision-card.tsx + inbox-screen.tsx)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Differentiate card weight at the list level by risk tier: financial and external-action items receive a 3px left-border accent in operator-amber; high-confidence routine items render with standard card weight. This implements the Brex 'exceptions clearly flagged' pattern — the operator's eye goes directly to the items that need thought without reading every card. The risk chip (already present) should move to the first visual element in the card hierarchy (top-left), not the bottom. Add a 'handled by [agent]' eyebrow in the agent's identity hue as the second element.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Presenting a 'send a follow-up message' item and a '$400 ad spend' item with identical visual weight forces the operator to read every card to understand stakes. Risk-sorted visual weight is the prerequisite for fast, confident triage — it is the information architecture decision that makes the swipe physics feel safe rather than reckless.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home 'Needs You' count + Home hero (home-page.tsx)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: The 'Needs You' count should decrement optimistically on every approval action (regardless of which surface the action happens on) via a shared React Query mutation that invalidates decisions.feed(). The Home loading state should use a layout-matched skeleton (three card-shaped placeholders, a headline-shaped placeholder) rather than the current no-isLoading-branch cold-start into the fallback 'we don't have a read on today' copy. The 'all clear' payoff state should be a crafted moment — not 'All caught up · 0 open enquiries' but a brief acknowledgment like 'You're all clear · Alex is watching' with the agent's ambient indicator.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The 'Needs You' count is the product's primary attention signal. A count that lags reality by 60s trains operators to ignore it. The cold-start into the fallback verdict reads as broken. The 'all clear' payoff is the emotional peak of the triage loop — it should feel like triumph, not like arriving at an empty store.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Loading states across authed routes (app/(auth)/)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add app/(auth)/loading.tsx with a warm-system skeleton layout (sidebar navigation placeholder + content area placeholder in hsl(45 25% 98%) with pulsing hsl(45 15% 92%) shimmer). For the Inbox specifically, fix the isLoading gate to use !data && !error (the keys-pending-safe check that Mira desk already uses correctly) to eliminate the false 'That's everything' empty state. The Inbox in-component loading state should use a set of 3 card-shaped skeletons matching the decision card layout rather than the bare unstyled 'Loading…' div.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The marketing page has a polished skeleton; the three highest-frequency authed surfaces have none. The Inbox false-empty (showing 'That's everything' before data arrives) is a critical trust failure — the operator believes they have no pending decisions, closes the app, and misses something that needed their attention. The isLoading fix is a 3-line change that eliminates this class of failure entirely.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Operator Chat widget (operator-chat-widget.tsx in auth layout)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Remove bg-blue-600 and the 'Operator Chat — Type a command' label from the globally-mounted widget, or relocate it entirely out of the main approval/triage flow surfaces. If the widget serves a legitimate operator use case, it should be accessible via a non-intrusive path (a keyboard shortcut, a dedicated settings/assistant surface) rather than floating on every approval screen in a developer-console aesthetic. The 'Type a command like show pipeline' framing actively contradicts the colleague metaphor on the surface where operators are making real money decisions.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A floating blue developer-console widget on the approval screen visually communicates 'you need to know commands to control this' at precisely the moment the operator needs to feel that the system is working for them. The warm-editorial colleague aesthetic and the developer console are mutually exclusive frames.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Keyboard model for Inbox (inbox-decision-card.tsx, desktop two-pane layout)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add role='button' tabIndex={0} onKeyDown (Enter/Space to open detail, A to approve, D to decline, S to snooze) to the decision card div. For desktop two-pane, add J/K navigation (following Linear and Superhuman's vim-inspired model) to move between items. This directly fixes the WCAG 2.1.1 keyboard failure on the core flow and, more importantly, enables power-user triage speed that approaches Superhuman's velocity on the primary daily surface.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The core approval flow is today pointer-only — no keyboard path exists. This is both a hard accessibility failure and a missed opportunity: medspa operators who are in the app daily will develop keyboard fluency if it exists, reducing their time-to-clear-queue. The pattern is established, the grammar is small (A/D/S/J/K), and it generalizes to desktop two-pane.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval grammar consistency across Home / Inbox card / detail sheet
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Standardize to four verbs, identical everywhere: Approve (amber primary), Decline (neutral with mandatory one-line note field), Defer (snooze picker), Escalate (flag for team review, maps to the existing human-escalation architecture). Every surface — the swipe gesture, the card action buttons, the detail sheet CTA, the keyboard shortcut — maps to the same four verbs. The mandatory decline note follows Linear's pattern: a single required field prevents silent dismissal and creates the audit trail that the operator's own lawyer would want.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Currently 'approve' has different behaviors and different labels in the drawer path vs. the main Inbox page vs. the detail sheet, and there is no keyboard equivalent anywhere. Operators who use all three surfaces must re-learn the interaction model each time. A consistent four-verb grammar, reinforced by keyboard shortcuts, is the prerequisite for the kind of muscle-memory triage speed that makes clearing a queue feel satisfying.

angle: Medspa/aesthetic-clinic and SMB owner-operator audience psychology: what earns their trust to hand AI marketing spend and customer communications to agents, the visual/brand language of their world, and what would delight vs alienate them — grounded in the specific audit gaps documented in Switchboard's current state.

principles.principle: Trust is not built by claiming it — it is earned through demonstrated, visible competence on small stakes first.
principles.why: JPMorgan Chase Institute research shows SMB owners move from experimentation to operational integration only after experiencing value incrementally. Deloitte's TrustID Index found trust in agentic AI collapsed 89% between May–July 2025, largely because systems claimed autonomous capability without building a track record. HBR data shows only 6% of organizations fully trust AI agents with core processes; the other 94% require supervised or routine-only use. For a medspa owner already anxious about rising patient acquisition costs (doubled since 2023) and the 35%/65% aspiration-vs-reality gap, a product that asks for full trust on day one is categorically rejected. The correct model is 'start supervised, show per-domain track records, celebrate milestones, and only offer autonomy upgrades when performance warrants it.'
principles.source: JPMorgan Chase Institute (2025 small business AI report), Deloitte TrustID Index cited in CIO.com (2025), HBR/Fortune survey (Dec 2025), AmericanMedSpa.org marketing investment gap report (2026)

principles.principle: Perceived control matters more than actual control — the visible ability to intervene is the trust signal, even if rarely used.
principles.why: UXmatters (2025) articulates this precisely: 'A system that allows intervention even if the user rarely needs it feels trustworthy.' Landbase's agentic AI trust checklist confirms: users click 'Start' when they believe they can correct course quickly and maintain meaningful control, not when they technically have it. For a medspa owner who is time-poor and mobile-first, this means always-visible pause/halt controls and undo with a countdown are more valuable than perfect AI accuracy. The Switchboard audit confirms the exact failure: the Undo safety net is an un-themed toast with 16-min delay that evicts on the next approval, and the 'already handled' stale-409 path is built but never wired — operators are technically protected but don't know it.
principles.source: UXmatters.com 'Designing for Autonomy' (Dec 2025), Landbase 'Building User Trust in Agentic AI' checklist, Smashing Magazine agentic AI UX patterns (Feb 2026)

principles.principle: The commitment moment — where real money or customer messages go out — must be the MOST finished surface, not a placeholder.
principles.why: For a medspa operator, every ad spend approval and every customer message carries personal stakes: their ad budget, their reputation with clients, their clinic's voice. Stripe's design philosophy (radical transparency, no fine print, no surprises) and fintech UX research confirm that financial trust moments require clear before/after states, projected outcomes, and plain language — not em-dashes labelled 'preview not yet wired.' The medspa world's own trust vocabulary ('transparency about results,' 'no hidden fees,' clinical proof through before/after') maps directly to this: operators running aesthetic clinics already know what a premium before/after commitment looks like. Shipping a placeholder in that slot communicates 'we don't respect your money.'
principles.source: Stripe payment UX gold standard (illustration.app), Fintech UX trust patterns (phenomenonstudio.com), medspa brand trust research (mangoeyesagency.com, colorlib medspa website examples 2026)

principles.principle: Calm, minimal, warm-editorial aesthetic is precisely right for this audience — but calm must be ALIVE, not inert.
principles.why: Medspa interior design research (theaestheticsyndicate.com) documents that premium aesthetic clinics use 'simplicity as trust signal' — warm neutrals, minimal clutter, intentional curation signal mastery and control. The same visual vocabulary translates directly to software. However, the calm technology framework (Weiser & Brown, 1995; caseorganic.com principles) specifies that calm technology must still 'move easily from periphery to center and back' — it informs without demanding attention, but it IS actively present. An agent that never moves, never acknowledges its own activity, and shows flat letter-discs instead of a breathing presence fails the 'alive' half of calm. The ambient presence display pattern (aiuxplayground.com) maps the solution: idle/active/completed states communicated through subtle, glanceable peripheral cues — like a Nest Hub ambient mode or macOS menu-bar indicator.
principles.source: theaestheticsyndicate.com medspa design psychology, caseorganic.com Principles of Calm Technology (Weiser/Brown 1995), aiuxplayground.com ambient presence displays pattern

principles.principle: Transparency is not a modal — it is a semantic layer that makes agent reasoning legible in plain language, always present at the right altitude.
principles.why: Brex's 'future of financial reporting' philosophy states: 'The future of reporting is not a chart. It's an insight.' The Smashing Magazine agentic AI UX guide (Feb 2026) specifies the 'explainable rationale' pattern: 'Because you said X, I did Y.' The aiuxdesign.guide trust calibration research shows confidence visualization is one of the top differentiators between AI products that retain users — but the signal is only valuable if calibrated (if 'high confidence' is wrong >5% of the time, the signal destroys trust). For a medspa operator who uses words like 'authenticity' and 'transparency about recovery' to evaluate clinical providers, a product that explains its actions in their language ('Alex paused this campaign because spend-per-booking exceeded your $45 cap') earns more trust than a product that merely shows data.
principles.source: Brex 'future of financial reporting is not a chart', Smashing Magazine agentic AI UX patterns (Feb 2026), aiuxdesign.guide trust calibration patterns

principles.principle: The operator's aesthetic world is warm, serif-editorial, premium-clean — not clinical white, not developer-console blue, not dashboard-gray.
principles.why: The medspa brand vocabulary (colorlib top medspa website examples, theaestheticsyndicate.com) is consistent: warm neutrals/taupe/cream, serif-sans typographic pairings (Playfair Display with modern sans), atmospheric photography, grid-based scannable organization, and above all the principle that 'luxury thrives on simplicity.' The operator's own business looks like this. When they open Switchboard and see a floating bg-blue-600 widget labelled 'Operator Chat — Type a command,' they are looking at developer infrastructure in their aesthetic treatment room. The entire wellness/beauty brand world (Tatcha, Alo Yoga, premium medspa sites) operates in the same warm-white, serif-accented, amber/gold-toned register that Switchboard's own design system uses — and the clash with developer-console elements is therefore proportionally more jarring for this audience than for any other.
principles.source: colorlib.com 29 Best Med Spa Website Examples (2026), theaestheticsyndicate.com interior design psychology, medspamarketing.com brand voice research

principles.principle: Time-poverty is structural, not situational — every interaction must be completable in a clinical break.
principles.why: Med spa workflow research (keragon.com) documents operators working between back-to-back treatments, fielding inquiries while managing clinical, operational, and administrative work simultaneously. Mangomint/Zenoti research confirms mobile is the primary access pattern. This maps to Superhuman's design manifesto (blakecrosley.com): the 50ms response threshold matters emotionally; optimistic UI actions complete visually before the server responds; and the J/K/E triage model (for today / another day / done) lets operators clear a decision queue in a clinical break without context-switching overhead. For Switchboard, this means: approving items on the Inbox page must immediately remove the card (optimistic, not waiting for server), the 'things need you' count must drop in the same motion, and the empty state must reward completion the way Superhuman's inbox-zero image does.
principles.source: keragon.com med spa workflow automations, Superhuman design study (blakecrosley.com), Zenoti medspa trends 2026

principles.principle: Fear of looking foolish outweighs fear of missing out — irreversible mistakes with customers are catastrophic to reputation.
principles.why: AestheticConversion.com and medspa branding research consistently emphasize that medspa clients choose providers based on trust, credentials, and personal referrals — and a single bad experience destroys that relationship. For an owner whose entire business is built on intimate, trust-based client relationships, an AI agent that sends a wrong message to a client is not a 'bug' — it is a reputation event. This explains why the risk-gated swipe safety model (Switchboard's canSwipeApprove() gating) is architecturally correct and must be VISIBLE to the operator, not just functionally present. The operator needs to SEE that the system routes high-stakes items to detail review; the behavior alone (without the visual communication) does not build trust.
principles.source: AestheticConversion.com medspa marketing research, mangoeyesagency.com 'Build Trust Not Trends', medspa consumer trust research (Zenoti)

principles.principle: Skeleton/loading states are a trust signal, not a performance optimization — a broken-looking load state reads as a broken product.
principles.why: For a time-poor operator opening the app between appointments, a bare 'Loading…' div or a cold-start fallback verdict ('we don't have a read on today') reads as the product being asleep or broken. The 2025 mobile UX research from Eleken (mobile UX design examples) shows that skeleton screens communicate 'the system is working' and reduce perceived wait time by setting structural expectations. More importantly, the false-empty-state bug (Inbox flashing 'That's everything' when approvals are pending) is experienced not as a performance issue but as a trust failure — the operator thinks they are caught up when they are not. This is the worst possible signal for a product whose value proposition is 'I keep you informed.'
principles.source: eleken.co mobile UX design examples (2026), mindBodyOnline medspa software research, keragon.com workflow automation

principles.principle: The product's 'soul' — the agent character — is the brand's primary differentiator and cannot be offstage.
principles.why: Medspa brand research (boundev.com wellness branding, theaestheticsyndicate.com) documents that personalization touchpoints — the curated music, the infused water, the staff who know your name — are retention mechanisms that create emotional bonds. For a software product whose agents are literally named Alex, Riley, and Mira and whose strongest copy moment is Alex's signed weeknote letter, having those agents appear as flat letter-discs that never move is the equivalent of decorating an aesthetic treatment room with stock photos of strangers. The aiuxplayground.com ambient presence research shows the solution exists: breathing aura, idle/active/completed state cues — present in periphery, moves to center only when needed. The 1,100+ lines of animated sprite code already built represents exactly the right instinct; it just needs to be connected.
principles.source: boundev.com wellness branding strategy, theaestheticsyndicate.com retention psychology, aiuxplayground.com ambient presence displays

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Speed as soul: 50ms response threshold makes actions feel anticipatory, not fast. Optimistic UI removes items immediately on commit (archive with E, undo with Z) — zero perceived latency. The triage model (J/K/E: for today / another day / done) is completable in 90 seconds between meetings. The inbox-zero completion moment — a rotating full-bleed photograph — is a genuine emotional reward for clearing the queue. Onboarding builds muscle memory through 30min 1:1 practice on synthetic emails before touching real ones, increasing shortcut adoption 20% and feature usage 67%. Visual design: color for status and focus only, never decoration. Source: blakecrosley.com Superhuman design study.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Inbox must immediately remove the approved card (optimistic), drop the 'things need you' count in the same frame, and make the cleared-queue state feel like a moment of genuine completion — not a static empty state. The triage physics already exist (swipe); the optimistic-update contract does not. The Superhuman model also validates that a premium product can charge for speed and feel as a primary value proposition, not just features.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Optimistic updates everywhere: the philosophy is 'network requests will most likely succeed — UI responsiveness should not depend on network latency.' Keyboard-first with a global command palette (Cmd+K) that simultaneously provides feature access and passively trains shortcuts. Minimalist aesthetic reduces cognitive load so primary work stays front and center. Speed as design decision, not just engineering: 'speed isn't just an engineering problem, it's a design problem too.' Issue removal is instant and uses FLIP animation so the list reflows with spatial continuity rather than snapping. Source: performance.dev Linear speed breakdown, skywork.ai Linear deep dive.
exemplars.takeaway: The Inbox decision card removal needs optimistic FLIP list animation — the card should leave within one frame of the commit gesture, the list should reflow with spatial continuity, and the feed and count should update in the same motion. This is a design commitment before an engineering one: the product must decide that list removal is instant by contract, then build it.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Radical transparency as trust infrastructure: '2.9% + 30¢ per transaction, no fine print, no surprises.' Real-time status pages showing system performance and outages. Public API documentation legible to non-developers. For payment flows: real-time validation as you type, card type detection, instant inline error feedback before submission. The philosophical posture is 'institutional honesty' — the system cannot lie to you about what it is doing or what it will cost. Source: illustration.app Stripe payment UX analysis, medium.com design bootcamp Stripe transparency article.
exemplars.takeaway: The 'What this changes' / 'Money at risk' approval panel is Switchboard's Stripe moment. It must show the real before/after derived from the existing Decision/recommendation payload — not em-dashes. Even a minimal honest panel (the action in plain English, the dollar amount from the payload, the reversibility window from undoableUntil) is categorically better than a placeholder. The Stripe principle is: never show a number you can't stand behind, but always show the real number.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: German engineering applied to personal productivity: 'designed not only to look fantastic, but to be perfectly functional.' Two Apple Design Awards. Micro-interactions, thoughtful typography, and a navigation structure that is deep but never confusing. Distraction-free: 'no distractions here, it's just you and your thoughts when you open a task.' The organizational structure (Areas, Projects, Headings) is deep enough for complex lives but the default view presents exactly what you need today. Visual design uses serif for headers, clean sans for body, generous white space. Source: ixd.prattsi.org Things 3 design critique, thedigitalprojectmanager.com review.
exemplars.takeaway: Things 3 validates Switchboard's three-noun primary IA (Home/Inbox/Results) — the courage to hide the forensic surfaces is correct. The specific lesson: depth is present but not on the default view. Switchboard's equivalent is making the daily surface (Home) complete enough that the operator does not need to drill into /activity or /reports to feel informed. The Home weeknote + agent panel already has this ambition; it needs the loading/empty states and cumulative trust anchor to complete it.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Approval workflows with conditional routing by amount threshold — $200/$1,000/$5,000/CFO — make the governance model legible to non-financial operators. Mobile-first review and approval. The philosophy articulated in their engineering blog: 'The future of reporting is not a chart. It's an insight.' AI surfaces anomalies and contextualizes findings against historical patterns rather than presenting raw data. A proprietary semantic layer translates raw database columns into financial concepts a senior analyst would recognize — the operator reads insights in their language, not SQL. Source: brex.com journal 'future of financial reporting is not a chart', brex.com expense approval process.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's Results surface and approval detail should speak in the operator's language: not 'CPM: $14.20' but 'Riley spent $14.20 to reach 1,000 people — last month it was $18.' The Brex model shows that tiered governance (amounts below threshold auto-approve, above require review) is legible and trusted when the thresholds are operator-set and visible. This maps directly to Switchboard's autonomy dial and the Conservative/Balanced/Aggressive modes.

exemplars.product: Premium medspa websites (sector exemplars: Aesthetica, Skin Laundry, RealSelf-networked practices)
exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: The best medspa websites share a specific visual vocabulary: warm neutral palettes (beige, warm taupe, cream) with one precious-metal accent; serif-sans typographic pairings (Playfair Display or Ivar Fine with geometric sans); editorial grid-based layout; atmospheric photography; before/after as primary conversion tool. 'Luxury thrives on simplicity — keeping the space visually clean and intentionally curated reinforces a sense of trust, calm, and high-end service before the client even speaks to your staff.' The strongest designs 'merge clinical credibility with spa-like calm — never clinical coldness, never spa-only vagueness.' Source: colorlib.com 29 Best Med Spa Website Examples (2026), theaestheticsyndicate.com.
exemplars.takeaway: Switchboard's warm-white canvas, one operator-amber action color, editorial serif, and agent hues for identity only is the exact right register for this audience — they live in this visual language professionally. The specific design lesson: the amber/gold accent must appear consistently and exclusively as the action color; the serif must anchor editorial moments (Home weeknote, results narrative); and the product must never drift into clinical cold white or developer-console blue. The floating bg-blue-600 'Operator Chat' widget is the single most jarring UX failure for this specific audience because it violates their professional visual world so completely.

antipatterns: Developer-console aesthetics on any operator-facing surface: bg-blue-600 floating widgets, 'Type a command', terminal-style prompts, or any surface that looks like infrastructure rather than a considered product. This audience runs businesses that look like spas — they viscerally reject interfaces that look like CLIs.

Placeholder trust moments: em-dashes in 'Money at risk: —' fields, 'preview not yet wired / wiring it up next week' labels, or any hint that the approval detail is incomplete. In this audience's world, a clinic that says 'we'll tell you the price later' loses the client instantly. The same reflex applies to software.

Trust theatre: visible safety controls (undo buttons, oversight dashboards, approval flows) that are not actually wired — the toast that never dismisses, the alreadyHandled path that silently closes, the 'safe' mode that has no mechanical difference from the unsafe one. Deloitte's TrustID data showing 89% trust collapse in agentic AI in a single quarter is driven exactly by discovered safety theatre.

Dark mode that activates automatically and breaks 70% of surfaces: for a mobile-first operator who opens the app on a dark-OS phone between appointments, arriving at black-ink-on-dark illegible text is not a dark-mode bug — it is a broken product. Ship light-only until a complete dark palette exists.

Stale state after action: approving an item and seeing the count unchanged, the card still present, or items reappearing on the next poll destroys the operator's mental model of what they just did. For someone managing between appointments, 'did that actually work?' is a trust-destroying question. The Inbox invalidation gap (main page not invalidating decisions.feed()) is the most acute instance.

Agent identity inconsistency: the same agent wearing 2–5 different faces, colors, and type treatments across adjacent screens (serif-letter-circle on Home vs hex pixel-sprite-square in AgentPanel vs hardcoded Mira violet in inbox-avatar). An operator who has invested trust in 'Alex' needs Alex to look and feel like the same entity everywhere — just as a medspa's brand identity is meticulously consistent across their own materials.

Optimization for acquisition at the cost of retention: the marketing landing with 100% 'illustrative' data and a mailto: CTA sells a product that doesn't exist to an operator who will arrive to something different. Medspa clients leave practices that overpromise. The same applies to software — the honest-marketing guard tests are exactly right and should be extended, not relaxed.

Loading states that look broken: bare 'Loading…' text, false-empty-zero states while data is pending, and cold-start fallback verdicts ('we don't have a read on today') on the most-opened screen. Time-poor operators read 'broken loading state' as 'broken product' and reduce usage before investigating.

Autonomy without track record: asking an operator to approve 'Go live' with zero passing simulated scenarios, or showing an agent operating at full autonomy before the operator has seen it succeed at supervised tasks. JPMorgan Chase Institute data shows SMB owners move to operational integration only after experiencing incremental value.

Generic 'AI dashboard' aesthetics: grey cards, blue CTAs, stat tiles with no narrative context, and data-dense layouts that replicate what the operator's existing booking software looks like. The medspa world's premium visual language is editorial, warm, and story-first — a product that looks like 'another dashboard' is competing in the wrong register.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox approval detail sheet (trust-critical commit moment)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace all em-dash placeholders in the 'What this changes' panel with the minimum honest data derivable from the existing Decision/recommendation payload today: the action in plain English ('Alex will send a rebooking message to 3 clients from last Tuesday'), the dollar figure from the payload where present ('$120 estimated ad spend'), and the reversibility window from undoableUntil ('Reversible for 8 minutes'). Wire the alreadyHandled stale-409 path to show a specific message: 'Already handled by [teammate name] · [N] minutes ago.' These are not design improvements — they are the difference between a commitment surface and a prototype. The Stripe principle applies: never show a number you can't stand behind, but always show the real number.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Only 6% of companies fully trust AI agents with core processes (HBR/Fortune 2025). For a medspa operator, the approval moment is the highest-stakes touch in the product — real money, real customer relationships. A placeholder in this slot communicates that the product does not respect the operator's money. The 'What this changes' panel is Switchboard's Stripe checkout: it must be the most finished surface, not the least.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox feed (optimistic update + count sync)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Implement the canonical onMutate optimistic removal for the main Inbox page: card disappears within one frame of the commit gesture (FLIP animation for spatial continuity), decisions.feed() is invalidated in the same mutation, and the Home 'things need you' count updates in the same React query invalidation cycle. Add layout-matched skeleton cards for the Inbox loading state, replacing the bare 'Loading…' div. Fix the isLoading gate to the !data && !error keys-pending-safe pattern already used in mira-desk-page.tsx:34. The cleared-queue state should be a designed moment — not a generic empty state — that acknowledges the operator's act of clearing work.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Superhuman's core design principle is that UI responsiveness cannot depend on network latency. For a medspa operator clearing approvals between appointments, approving an item and seeing the count unchanged is a trust failure — 'did that actually work?' is exactly the question the product must never make them ask. The optimistic-update model already works in the header-drawer path; bringing the main Inbox page to parity is correcting a regression, not adding a feature.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Undo safety net (toast)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Redesign the Undo flow using three specific fixes: (1) Replace the un-themed shadcn toast with the branded .toast-undo styling that already exists in the codebase. (2) Bind the countdown to the real undoableUntil window from the payload, not a fixed delay — show a live '7 minutes to undo' timer so the operator understands the actual window. (3) Change TOAST_LIMIT from 1 to at least 3 so sequential approvals each get their own reversible safety net instead of silently evicting the previous one. The undo button must be keyboard-accessible. Frame the UI copy as 'Done — undo until [time]' not a generic 'Action completed.'
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The visible ability to intervene is the trust signal even if rarely used (UXmatters 2025). A toast that auto-evicts after one approval communicates that the system is not actually watching out for the operator. For a medspa owner whose business reputation depends on never sending the wrong message to a client, 'I can undo this' must be visibly true — not technically true and practically invisible.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Dark mode toggle (Settings → Account)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Hide the Dark and System options from the Settings → Account toggle immediately — ship it as light-only. The rationale must be internal: only ~40 of ~145 tokens have .dark values; all editorial, agent-identity, shadow, Mercury, and cockpit tokens have none; and the System option auto-activates on dark-OS phones with zero user action. A medspa operator opening the app on a Samsung Galaxy in dark mode and seeing black-ink-on-dark text does not diagnose 'incomplete dark-mode tokens' — they think the product is broken. Reintroduce the toggle only after a complete .dark palette is implemented across all token families, and specifically after the cockpit T-token system is rewritten to use hsl(var(--...)) the way mira-config.ts already demonstrates.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The shipped Light/Dark/System toggle breaks approximately 70% of surfaces (audit-verified). The calm-technology principle (Weiser/Brown) states: 'Think about what happens if your technology fails — does it default to a usable state or completely break down?' The current system defaults to breaking down. Ship-light-only is the only honest choice until the foundation is complete.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Agent identity and presence (Home, Inbox, AgentPanel, cockpits)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Establish ONE canonical agent avatar representation at each display size — breathing OperatorCharacter for hero contexts, ambient aura indicator for list/row contexts — and wire it to real agent status (busy/idle/halted → working/idle/sleep). The AgentPanel hardcode of state='idle' must be replaced with a live status derivation. The same agent must wear the same face, the same identity hue (one value, not 2–5 conflicting ones), and the same type treatment everywhere. Specifically: standardize Alex's identity color to one value across globals.css, tailwind config, and all component-level overrides; do the same for Mira (hsl(270 45% 58%) canonical, per the cockpit token system note). The floating bg-blue-600 'Operator Chat' widget must be re-skinned to use operator-amber and the product's type system, or removed from the global layout entirely if it is not ready for operators.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Medspa interior design research shows that personalization touchpoints — the staff who know your name, the curated details — are the retention mechanisms that create emotional bonds. Alex's signed weeknote letter is the product's best single moment precisely because it makes the agent feel like a real colleague. An agent that wears 2–5 different faces one tap apart, never moves, and is hardcoded to 'idle' is not a colleague — it is a widget. The ambient presence pattern (aiuxplayground.com) provides the right model: idle/active/completed states through low-attention peripheral cues, earning center-of-attention only when the operator needs to engage.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home (daily emotional anchor)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add three specific fixes: (1) A proper loading skeleton for the Home page — layout-matched, warm-neutral skeleton that communicates structure before data, using the !data && !error keys-pending-safe gate. (2) A 'since you hired Alex' cumulative trust anchor on the Home verdict — the agent panel already has this ('since you hired, Alex has handled X leads totalling $Y') and it is the single most trust-building metric in the product; surface it as the Home hero's long-horizon number alongside the 7-day window. (3) Remove the hardcoded empty workInProgressItems and replace with either live data or a designed empty state that doesn't look broken. The first-run useFirstRun checklist should be wired to the post-launch Home as the post-activation experience, replacing the 'All caught up · 0 enquiries' empty state.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: American MedSpa Association data shows 35% of practices experienced no growth despite expecting it — owners' anxiety is rooted in not knowing if their investments are compounding. The 'since you hired' framing reframes Alex from a feature into an investment that has been working on their behalf for months. Calm technology (Weiser/Brown) requires information to be present in the periphery and move to center when needed — a Home that cold-starts into 'we don't have a read on today' is not calm, it is absent.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval/commitment copy and trust-critical microcopy system
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Codify the voice already present in the product's strongest surfaces into an explicit lexicon: 'Later' not 'Skip' on money decisions; name the irreversible consequence specifically ('This message will be sent to 3 clients — cannot be recalled'); use 'because' to explain agent reasoning ('Alex suggested this because spend-per-booking is trending above your $45 target'); never collapse error into 'all clear'; never invent a number that isn't computed from real data. The Smashing Magazine agentic AI pattern for 'explainable rationale' is: 'Because you said X, I did Y.' Apply this pattern to every approval card that currently shows a category label and a dollar amount without a sentence of explanation.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Medspa clients trust providers who explain their reasoning ('you're a good candidate for this treatment because of X, but we'd want to avoid Y'). The same vocabulary earns trust in software for this audience. The aiuxdesign.guide trust calibration research is explicit: 'I'm very confident about this' vs 'I'm guessing here' — calibrated honesty is a top differentiator between AI products that retain users and those that don't. The product's own voice guidelines (already encoded in the honest-marketing guard tests) should be extended to every approval surface.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Results surface (ROI narrative and negative-delta visibility)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Two specific fixes derived from Brex's 'insight not a chart' philosophy: (1) Make the worsening ROI delta visually distinct from flat — deltaNeg at ink-3 (~3.6:1 contrast) is near-invisible against the warm-white background; darken it to a muted but perceptible color distinct from deltaFlat without using alarm red (the medspa world avoids medical alarm colors in their own branding). A warm charcoal or slightly cooler neutral works. (2) Add a one-sentence narrative context line below each primary KPI: not 'CPM: $14.20' but 'Riley reached 1,000 people for $14.20 — $3.80 less than last month.' This is the Brex semantic layer applied to ad performance: translate raw metrics into operator-language insights.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: 35% of medspa owners now cite ROI and performance as their dominant marketing decision factor (AmericanMedSpa.org 2026). A Results surface where a worsening trend looks identical to a flat one is not a results surface — it is a data dump. The operator cannot make the ROI judgment that justifies their continued investment in the product.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Marketing landing (top of funnel CTA and credibility)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace the mailto: CTA with a link to the actual (auth)/onboarding flow that exists in the codebase — the infrastructure for self-serve is already built but never linked. Fix the doubled-word typo ('One you you don't recognize'). Add ONE verifiable proof anchor that the honest-marketing guard tests will permit: either a real pilot quote with clinic name and city, or an 'N aesthetic practices' count if that number is real. The Landbase agentic AI trust checklist notes that 'preview dashboards showing high-level statistics before execution' are the key to getting operators to click Start — the Riley live-faux-Meta-Ads beat and the Mira pipeline beat already do this and are described in the audits as the best proof sections in the product. Lead with those beats.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Patient acquisition costs have doubled since 2023 (AmericanMedSpa.org); medspa owners are already suspicious of marketing spend that doesn't produce verifiable ROI. A landing page that is 100% 'illustrative' and ends in a mailto: applies the same anxiety to the product itself. The operator asking 'can I trust this?' deserves at least one answer they can verify.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Loading states and skeleton system (Home, Inbox, Results — the three daily authed surfaces)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add route-level loading.tsx shells for all three daily authed surfaces using layout-matched skeleton content in the warm-neutral palette. The skeletons must match the actual content structure (not generic grey bars) so the operator's eye can orient to where the weeknote, the KPI tiles, and the approval cards will appear. Extract the AgentPanel three-states invariant (loading/error/empty each own their slot, error never reads as 0, zero shown dimmed-but-present) into a shared QueryStates primitive usable across all three surfaces. Apply the !data && !error keys-pending-safe loading gate everywhere isLoading is currently used as the gate. This is a system-level commitment, not a per-page fix.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Mobile UX research shows skeleton screens reduce perceived wait time by communicating structure before content. For a time-poor operator opening the app between appointments, a skeleton that orients them to incoming information is the difference between 'the product is working' and 'the product is broken.' The false-empty-state bug (Inbox showing 'That's everything' while approvals are pending) is the most trust-damaging instance — an operator who clears their queue based on false-empty information and then discovers missed approvals will not trust the product again.

angle: 2026 premium-software visual craft: warm-editorial, Claude-inspired, and actionable for Switchboard's specific floor/ceiling gap

principles.principle: Semantic token architecture, not parallel hex silos
principles.why: Hardcoded hex values guarantee three failure modes that are all present in Switchboard today: color drift when the same hue is defined multiple times with conflicting values, inability to theme or dark-mode any component that references a literal, and a cascade where any color fix must be applied N times or it silently regresses. The correct architecture is three tiers: primitive tokens (raw HSL values, e.g. --primitive-amber-500: 30 55% 46%), semantic tokens (role decisions, e.g. --action-primary: var(--primitive-amber-500)), and component tokens (local overrides). Components reference only semantic tokens; semantic tokens reference primitives; primitives are the sole source of truth. This is how Linear, Stripe, and Material 3 all operate. The migration path is mechanical not creative: rewrite each literal in cockpit/tokens.ts to hsl(var(--...)) exactly as mira-config.ts already demonstrates. The lint rule comes last but makes the new architecture stick: a CI check that errors on any raw hex or rgba in a component file.
principles.source: Muzli Design Blog, dark-mode-design-systems-a-complete-guide; CSS-Tricks design token guidance; Material Design 3 token docs; Penpot developer guide to design tokens; Medium/@andresclavijo 'Design Tokens Are Not CSS Variables'

principles.principle: Elevation via tonal luminance, not shadow accumulation
principles.why: Shadows become invisible on dark surfaces and lose coherence when sourced from four different base colors. The modern approach (Material 3, Linear, Mercury) separates the concept of elevation from its light-mode implementation. The system: define 4-5 named surface levels (surface-base, surface-raised, surface-overlay, surface-elevated, surface-modal) each differing by a 4-6% lightness step in light mode and by a 4-6% luminance increase in dark mode. Reserve real box-shadow for interactive lift only (cards on hover, popovers appearing), using a single base color at one opacity (e.g. hsla(30, 20%, 15%, 0.08) for the warm canvas). This replaces 35 ad-hoc shadows with a 4-token ladder and makes dark mode possible because tonal shift has a dark-mode counterpart (lighter-on-darker) where shadow does not.
principles.source: Material Design 3 tonal surfaces blog; designsystems.surf elevation design patterns article; Muzli dark mode complete guide; Mercury design system analysis

principles.principle: Ship light-only until dark mode is architecturally complete
principles.why: A broken dark mode is worse than no dark mode. On a mobile-first product, 'system' preference can activate dark mode on first load with zero user action, immediately rendering Home, Mira desk, and every cockpit as black-ink-on-dark — an illegible state that signals a broken product to the operator who just paid. The correct sequence: (1) disable or hide the System/Dark toggle behind a feature flag at launch; (2) complete the token unification so every value is var()-based; (3) write the full .dark block covering all ~145 tokens including editorial (warm near-black replaces cream), agent identity (maintain hue, shift lightness 50→65), shadows (replace with tonal steps), and cockpit (now var()-based so it participates); (4) test on both OLED and LCD; (5) re-enable the toggle. Shipping a 40/145 dark token coverage with a live toggle is a documented liability, not an aesthetic shortcoming.
principles.source: WebAIM April 2025 dark mode guidance; Muzli complete dark mode guide; Linear dark-mode breakdown analysis (Chyshkala); Superhuman benefits of dark mode blog

principles.principle: Optimistic-first with a branded, bounded undo net
principles.why: Superhuman's core insight — treat speed as the product, assume success unless proved otherwise — is directly applicable to Switchboard's approval loop. The pattern: on commit, remove the card immediately from the DOM (optimistic removal via onMutate), invalidate decisions.feed() so the count updates in the same tick, show an undo toast with a real 5-second countdown and visual progress bar. The toast is the safety net, not the loading state. If the server errors, restore the card with a brief shake animation and an honest error message. This pattern converts the 'card freezes for the network round-trip' experience into a 50ms interface that feels like it anticipates the action. Discord's reverse-loading-bar toast (hoverable to pause) is the closest production exemplar. The key constraint: TOAST_LIMIT must be queue-not-evict (never 1), and the countdown must be bound to the real undoableUntil window from the API payload.
principles.source: Superhuman design analysis (blakecrosley.com); Discord undo toast pattern; UX Collective snackbar/toast guidelines; Material Design 3 snackbar guidelines; Medium/@triplem656 AnimatePresence guide

principles.principle: Layout-matched skeletons everywhere data is pending, not spinners or bare text
principles.why: Skeleton screens reduce perceived load time by 20-40% compared to spinners because they communicate progress rather than just activity — users see the shape of what is coming, so the interface feels occupied rather than blank. For an operator who opens Switchboard daily to triage their queue, a bare 'Loading...' string on the Inbox is a trust signal about product quality. The craft requirement: skeletons must mirror the exact layout of the content they precede (same card heights, same number of rows as a typical feed, same column widths), must use the warm palette not neutral-gray (shimmer that travels from hsl(45 25% 94%) to hsl(45 25% 98%) on the warm canvas background), and must respect prefers-reduced-motion by using a static placeholder instead of shimmer. Route-level loading.tsx shells (one per authed route) are the lowest-cost highest-impact implementation because Next.js's App Router shows them within one frame of navigation.
principles.source: LogRocket skeleton loading screen UX design; Clay.global skeleton screens article; Medium Next.js loading.js + React Suspense; Onething.design skeleton vs spinner decision

principles.principle: Single action color, semantically reserved, AA-passing at rest
