principles.why: Operator-amber is the correct bet: it is warm, distinctive, and reserved for the ONE question an operator answers ('do I approve this?'). The problem is the current hsl(30 55% 46%) achieves only 3.76:1 against white text — below AA for normal-size text. The fix is a one-stop lightness reduction: hsl(30 58% 38%) clears AA at 4.65:1 on white while remaining visibly warm amber. The broader principle: a single action color only works when it is absolutely not used for anything else. Operator-amber must not appear on agent identity marks, informational badges, or decorative elements. Blue — which appears today as a raw bg-blue-600 on the Operator Chat widget — must be removed from the authed app entirely. The identity hues (Alex coral, Riley amber, Mira violet) must appear only on identity marks, never as action affordances. This three-way separation (amber=action, hue=identity, red=error) is how premium fintech avoids color ambiguity in the moments that matter.
principles.source: WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios; Orange You Accessible mini case study (Bounteous); Eleken fintech design trust patterns; AllAccessible WCAG 2025 guide; Fintech design guide (Themasterly)

principles.principle: Warm grain at low opacity removes digital flatness without decorating
principles.why: The warm-cream canvas (hsl 45 25% 98%) reads as premium but flat. Adding a fine SVG fractalNoise grain at 3-5% opacity over the base background layer is the technique behind Arc Browser's background warmth and is used across premium editorial products. The grain does not compete with content because it is below visual threshold at normal viewing distance, but it is perceptually present — the difference is felt as 'this feels crafted' rather than seen. The implementation is a single lightweight SVG data URI applied as a background-blend-mode: overlay layer at 4% opacity. Critical constraint: the grain must not appear on interactive elements (cards, buttons, inputs) — only on the page background and large surface areas. Removing it from dark mode is trivial since the warm-cream surface disappears. This is unambiguously the highest ROI-to-effort decorative technique: one CSS rule, zero dependencies.
principles.source: CSS-Tricks grainy gradients; FreeCodeCamp SVG noise backgrounds; Arc browser grain technique documented (WebSearch); ibelick.com creating grainy backgrounds CSS

principles.principle: Motion earns its place: FLIP for list exits, spring for arrivals, zero for commits
principles.why: Framer Motion's layout animation (FLIP via CSS transform: translate + scale, not width/height so no paint triggers) handles the Inbox card removal gracefully: add layout prop to each card, wrap the list in AnimatePresence, and the sibling cards rearrange in ~300ms with a spring easing while the removed card slides out. This is Superhuman's and Linear's approach. The critical complementary rule is zero blocking delay on the commit itself — the card removal happens in onMutate (optimistic), not on server response. The other half: prefers-reduced-motion already in Switchboard's codebase should bypass FLIP and snap-close, which is correct. Three timing values cover the whole system: 0ms (commit response), 120ms (short microinteractions like focus rings, button states), 300ms (FLIP list reorder, sheet entrance). Framer Motion is already installed with zero usages — the only cost is writing the 10-line AnimatePresence wrapper.
principles.source: Motion.dev React AnimatePresence and layout animation docs; Superhuman animation philosophy; Theodore Clarence list animation guide; Max Heckel advanced animation patterns

principles.principle: Real tabular figures for every number that column-aligns
principles.why: font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums is a single CSS declaration but its absence is perceptually loud: KPI numbers jitter as values update and column-scan is impossible because digits take variable widths. Inter (Switchboard's body font) does support tabular figures. The data-tabular attribute approach is a no-op if the CSS rule that should consume it is absent — the correct fix is a global rule applied directly to the semantic class not an attribute hook. The premium treatment for hero metrics: combine tabular-nums with lining-nums (not old-style figures), apply the editorial serif (Source Serif 4) at optical-size for the 56px hero numerals on Home's 'since you hired' anchor, and use Inter with tabular-nums for all KPI tiles, result tables, and money values. This is a one-line CSS fix that converts every data surface from 'prototype quality' to 'professional instrument quality'.
principles.source: Tailwind CSS font-variant-numeric docs; MDN font-variant-numeric; Craig Francies tabular numbers article; Yellowfin BI dashboard typography best practice; WebToolsHub CSS design tokens 2026

principles.principle: Single global :focus-visible rule with branded amber ring
principles.why: WebAIM's 2024 analysis found 78% of web pages have detectable focus indicator issues. Switchboard's keyboard gap is both an accessibility failure (the Inbox decision card is a pointer-only div with no tabIndex or role, a hard WCAG 2.1.1 failure on the core flow) and a trust signal: a product that asks operators to trust AI with their money must itself behave with the rigor of a trustworthy tool. The global rule pattern: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid hsl(30 58% 38%); outline-offset: 2px; border-radius: inherit; } applied in globals.css replaces all four ad-hoc focus rules with one consistent appearance. The Inbox card requires tabIndex={0}, role='button', and onKeyDown handlers for Enter/Space/a/e (approve/dismiss). This is not optional craft — it is the gap between 'premium' and 'inaccessible'.
principles.source: WCAG 2.2 criterion 2.4.7 and 2.4.13; MDN :focus-visible; Level Access keyboard navigation guide; Vispero focus indicator guidance; WebAIM contrast and color 2025

principles.principle: The trust-critical confirmation panel must show the minimum honest preview, not a placeholder
principles.why: The 'What this changes / Money at risk' panel shipping literal em-dashes labeled 'preview not yet wired' is the product's single most damaging trust failure, because it occurs at the exact moment an operator is deciding whether to trust AI with a real financial or customer-facing action. The standard set by Stripe's confirmation UI and Superhuman's undo pattern is: show exactly what you know, state honestly what you cannot yet compute, never fabricate. For Switchboard: derive the visible fields from the existing Decision/recommendation payload (action type, agent, estimated spend, target entity) and show those. If the dollar impact cannot be computed, label it 'Estimated spend: calculating...' with a spinner, not '—'. If undo is available, show 'Undoable until [time]' from the real undoableUntil field. The 'already handled by teammate' 409 path should surface as 'Alex already handled this · 4 minutes ago' not silent close. This is a data-wiring task, not a design task — the design principle is: honest > empty.
principles.source: Stripe Checkout UI strategies; Eleken fintech trust patterns; Superhuman optimistic UI principles; UX Files notification UX; Medium fintech design guide

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Treats perceived speed as the core product feature. Internally targets 50-60ms for all primary actions, achieved through optimistic UI that removes the item before server confirmation and uses an undo toast with a 5-second countdown as the safety net. Every action produces an immediate visual response — there are no loading states on the primary flow. The typography system (Messina family, tight letter-spacing on subjects, fixed sizes for metadata) creates instant hierarchy without decoration. Their undo toast has a visual progress bar that pauses on hover, borrowed from Discord's reverse-loading-bar pattern.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: remove the Inbox card optimistically in onMutate, not on server success. The toast countdown is the reversal contract, not a spinner. This pattern applies directly to the #1 audit finding: the card freezing on commit is a solved problem in 2025.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Achieves a 'calm and alive simultaneously' feel through restraint rather than reduction. Uses a near-monochrome palette with a single purple accent, strong typographic hierarchy in Inter (tight tracking on nav items, loose on body), and FLIP layout animations on every list so item removal is a choreographed rearrangement rather than a snap. Their dark mode is designed first — every surface level is a luminance step upward, not a shadow, which is why it works on OLED. Their command palette doubles as a progressive keyboard-training surface.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the FLIP AnimatePresence wrapper on the Inbox feed is the Linear equivalent — 10 lines of code that converts snap-removal into a professional instrument. The luminance-step dark mode architecture is the direct template for completing Switchboard's .dark token block.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Masters the 'this is a real action with real consequences' moment through layout and microcopy discipline. Every confirmation surface shows a structured breakdown: what is changing, the exact amount, what happens next. Shadows are soft and diffuse (rgba 0,0,0,0.2 at 32px spread) with a single warm base — they read as light, not weight. Typography uses negative letter-spacing (-0.03em) on display text, creating a tight premium feel. Error messages are plain-language and prescriptive ('Check your card info') not vague ('Something went wrong').
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the 'What this changes' panel should follow the Stripe pattern: structured rows of exactly what is known (action type, agent, spend estimate, target entity), honest unknowns labeled 'calculating...' not '—', and a plain-English consequence line at the bottom.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Elevates through opacity and color shift rather than shadow. Their spacing scale (4/8/12/16/20/24/32/40/56/72) is a 4pt-base system that produces airy layouts without wasted space. Body type at a non-standard 420 weight acts as a quiet brand signature. They use progressive disclosure for sensitive data (account numbers hidden by default, tap to reveal) as a trust signal — the act of requiring a deliberate action communicates that data access is guarded.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: adopt the 4pt spacing scale explicitly (replacing the free-hand px mix) and codify it in Tailwind. The progressive-disclosure pattern applies to the approval detail: the full spend breakdown should require a tap to expand, making 'I saw the consequences' a physical act, not passive reading.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Won Apple's Design Award for applying genuine craft to a productivity surface: the 'Magic Plus' button drags to insert at the exact right position, transitions are spring-physics-based (task opening smoothly scales up rather than fading), and the typography system uses tight tracking on completed items to signal state without color changes. Most importantly: the app makes triage feel like action, not like admin. Every motion is purposeful and bounded.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the Inbox is a triage tool exactly like Things. The swipe physics Switchboard already built (rubber-band, axis-lock) is the Things equivalent — extend that same care to the card arrival and exit animations. The philosophy is: motion at the moments of decision, stillness everywhere else.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: The most instructive example of grain texture done correctly: Arc applies a fine fractalNoise SVG grain at roughly 4% opacity over the gradient background, creating warmth without a visible pattern. The result reads as 'intentionally crafted' rather than 'has texture.' Combined with a warm sidebar background (not white, not gray — a tinted near-neutral), it makes a browser feel like a premium object. The grain is invisible at arm's length but its absence would be felt.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: one CSS rule adding an SVG fractalNoise layer at 4% opacity over the warm-cream base is the highest-ROI craft upgrade available. It converts the flat cream canvas into something that reads as premium editorial product.

antipatterns: Hardcoded hex values in component files — guarantees color drift across multiple parallel definitions of the same brand color, makes dark mode impossible for those components, and means every color fix must be applied N times. The existence of cockpit/tokens.ts as 18 literal hex values is the root cause of Switchboard's color incoherence.

Four parallel design systems coexisting — globals.css HSL vars, hardcoded-hex T cockpit, Mercury register, v6 landing palette. Each addition erodes the others' authority. The cringe version is when the brand's own accent color for the marketing landing (coral 14 75% 55%) differs from the in-app action color (amber 30 55% 46%) and the operator experiences a visual brand switch the moment they sign in.

Shipping a mode toggle (Light/Dark/System) before dark mode tokens are complete — the 'System' option auto-activates on dark-OS phones with zero user action, turning a shipped but incomplete feature into a first-load liability. The antipattern is treating dark mode as a color inversion rather than a full second-theme that requires its own editorial, elevation, and identity token pass.

Raw bg-blue-600 (or any out-of-system color) in a globally-mounted component — the Operator Chat widget's blue is the most visible version of this, reframing the entire product as 'developer console' while every other surface says 'warm professional colleague'. Any color that does not come from the token system is a trust fracture.

Placeholder copy shipping in production trust-critical surfaces — em-dashes labeled 'preview not yet wired / wiring it up next week' in the approval detail panel is the highest-severity version, because it appears at the exact moment an operator is deciding whether to trust AI with their money. Placeholder copy signals 'prototype quality' and cannot be explained away by good design elsewhere.

TOAST_LIMIT=1 with a 16-minute removal delay for undo — a second approval evicts the first undo, making the safety net disappear without feedback. The correct model is a queue, not a single slot, and a real countdown (5-7 seconds or bound to undoableUntil) not an arbitrary near-infinite delay.

isLoading-gated loading states without handling the keys-pending state — the Inbox false-empty ('That's everything') flash is caused by checking isLoading instead of !data && !error. The antipattern is believing a loading gate prevents blank-state flash; keys-pending is neither loading nor error so it passes through both.

Pastel gradients + pill toggles + illustration fluff as a substitute for editorial craft — the visual fingerprint of a commodity SaaS product in 2025. Every SaaS dashboard started to look the same with this combination. Switchboard's warm-editorial restraint is the correct counter-bet; the antipattern would be adding gradient hero cards, rounded-pill tags on everything, and decorative illustration spots to 'liven up' the interface.

Excessive motion on commit — adding a loading spinner or progress animation to the approval action is the wrong response to the 'card freezes' complaint. The correct response is optimistic removal. Any blocking animation on the primary action path trains operators that this product is slow.

free-hand pixel spacing (7/9/11/14/18/22 mixed with 8/16/24) with dead --space-* tokens — this produces layouts that never settle into a visual rhythm. The antipattern is not the mix itself but having a token system that no component consumes, making the stated design language invisible in the actual product.

Serif-less cockpit type while the rest of the app uses editorial serif — the agent cockpit rendering agent name/title in generic Inter while Inbox uses Newsreader-italic for the same agent's voice creates a brand split where the agent's own home surface is the least crafted. The antipattern is treating cockpit as a 'technical surface exempt from editorial standards'.

Agent avatar state hardcoded to 'idle' — shipping 1,100+ lines of animated agent sprite code that is never activated because AgentPanel hardcodes state='idle' is worse than not building the animation: it proves the product has living agents that the team chose to freeze. Operators who notice the disconnect lose trust in the product's intentionality.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Token system foundation (cockpit/tokens.ts + globals.css + tailwind.config.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Collapse all four token systems onto one in a single mechanical migration PR. Rewrite every literal hex in tokens.ts to hsl(var(--...)) following the mira-config.ts pattern exactly. Audit the live footprint first: confirm ApprovalCard and roi-bar are dead code before migrating them. Add a CI lint rule (ESLint no-restricted-syntax or a simple grep in a pre-commit hook) that errors on any hex literal or rgba() in a .tsx or .ts component file. The three-tier architecture: primitive tokens in globals.css (raw HSL values), semantic tokens (role-based, e.g. --action-primary, --surface-raised), component tokens only where truly local. Resolve all agent identity hue conflicts to single canonical values verified against the live DB seed. This is the keystone that unblocks every other fix.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Parallel token systems guarantee that every color/dark-mode/a11y fix regresses elsewhere. Switchboard has three documented instances of the same hue defined with conflicting values. No dark mode, no a11y fix, and no theme work can succeed without a single source of truth. The migration is mechanical (find-replace with verification), not creative.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Settings → Account dark mode toggle
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Immediately hide the System and Dark options behind a feature flag (NEXT_PUBLIC_DARK_MODE_ENABLED=false). Show only Light. In parallel, write the full .dark token block for all ~145 tokens: warm near-black for editorial backgrounds (hsl 45 8% 10% for base, hsl 45 8% 13% for raised), agent identity hues at 60-65% lightness (up from 55-58% in light), editorial text as hsl 45 15% 90%, shadows replaced with tonal steps, and the cockpit (now var()-based) participating automatically. Test on both iPhone OLED and a standard LCD before re-enabling. The System preference auto-activation on dark-OS phones is the current liability.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: ~40 of ~145 dark tokens currently have values. A live toggle with this coverage renders Home, Mira desk, and every cockpit as illegible black-on-dark. The Linear principle applies: if you ship dark mode, ship it at equal quality to light, or do not ship it. Hiding the toggle costs nothing; an operator on a dark phone encountering a broken home screen costs trust.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox decision card (inbox-decision-card.tsx + useRecommendationAction + use-toast.ts)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Wire the full Superhuman-pattern commit loop: (1) Add onMutate to useRecommendationAction that immediately removes the card from the local decisions.feed() cache and removes it from the DOM via AnimatePresence; (2) Invalidate decisions.feed() (not just recommendations.all) in onMutate so the Needs-You count updates in the same tick; (3) Replace TOAST_LIMIT=1 with a queue model and TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY=5000 (5s real countdown); (4) Design the undo toast to match the warm system — amber-tinted background, countdown progress bar using Framer Motion's animate prop, 'Undo · 5s' label; (5) Wire the alreadyHandled 409 path to surface 'Already handled by your team · [relative time]' rather than silent close. Add AnimatePresence + layout prop to the card list so sibling cards animate into their new positions on removal (FLIP, 280ms spring).
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The 'card freezes on commit' and 'feed doesn't update' findings are the two most-cited critical audit gaps. The Superhuman pattern (optimistic remove → undo net → server confirm → error restore) is a 2025 standard for triage UIs. The TOAST_LIMIT=1 bug means the undo net disappears on the second action — this is a direct safety regression, not a polish gap.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet (approval-detail-sheet.tsx lines 203-235)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace every em-dash placeholder with the minimum honest panel derived from the existing Decision payload: show action type, agent name, target entity (customer or ad set), and estimated spend as a labeled row ('Estimated spend: $X.XX' or 'Estimated spend: computing...' with an inline spinner if not yet available from the payload). Add 'Undoable until [formatted time from undoableUntil]' in small text below the primary CTA. If undoableUntil is null, show 'This action cannot be undone' in a muted warning chip. Follow the Stripe layout principle: structured breakdown rows, honest unknown states, plain-English consequence line. Do not ship a 'Money at risk: —' label — either compute it or omit it; a labeled blank teaches operators that fields are unreliable.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The approval detail is the highest-stakes surface in the product: the moment an operator commits real money or real customer communications. Shipping placeholder copy ('wiring it up next week') there is the product's single most visible trust failure. The data to populate these fields already exists in the Decision payload — this is wiring, not design work.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Authed route loading states (app/(auth)/loading.tsx, inbox-screen.tsx, home-page.tsx, results page)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a (auth)/loading.tsx route-level shell using Next.js App Router conventions — this displays within one frame of navigation. Design it as a layout-matched skeleton: the same sidebar nav (fully rendered, not skeleton), the same page header zone (skeleton), and 3-4 card-shaped skeleton rows in the content area. Use a warm shimmer: @keyframes shimmer sweeping from hsl(45 25% 94%) to hsl(45 25% 98%) at 1.8s ease-in-out infinite. Replace inbox-screen.tsx's isLoading gate with the !data && !error keys-pending-safe pattern (copied from mira-desk-page.tsx line 34 which already has the correct fix). Add a skeleton to home-page.tsx behind the same gate so cold-start never shows the 'we don't have a read on today' fallback verdict before data has been attempted.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Only the public marketing route has a polished skeleton. The three highest-frequency authed surfaces (Home, Inbox, Results) have either no loading state or a bare unstyled string. This asymmetry — more craft on acquisition than on the daily loop — sends the wrong signal about where the product's investment lies. The warm shimmer uses the existing canvas color as base, not generic gray.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Operator Chat widget (operator-chat-widget.tsx, mounted in (auth)/layout.tsx)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Remove bg-blue-600 immediately — this is a color not in the warm system and it visually declares 'developer console' on every authed screen. If the widget must remain, restyle it using the warm token system: background hsl(var(--surface-raised)), border hsl(var(--border)), label uses the same voice as the rest of the product (not 'Type a command like "show pipeline"' — consider 'Ask Alex anything' aligned with the agent persona). Ideally, evaluate whether a globally-floating widget is the right architecture or whether this capability should live in the agent panel sheet. The blue widget is a category error: it makes every screen say 'developer tool' while everything else says 'trustworthy colleague'.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The widget appears on every authed screen. Its blue color is documented in the audit as the clearest single token-system violation visible to all operators at all times. The restyle is a 5-minute CSS fix; the architectural question about whether a floating command widget is right for a non-technical mobile-first operator is the deeper question.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Primary action button (operator-amber across Inbox, approval sheet, onboarding)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Darken the action color from hsl(30 55% 46%) to hsl(30 58% 38%) — this achieves 4.65:1 contrast against white text (passing WCAG AA for normal text) while remaining unmistakably warm amber. Update --operator (or --action-primary in the new semantic token) to this single value across the full token system. Add a :focus-visible global rule in globals.css: *:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid hsl(30 58% 38%); outline-offset: 2px; border-radius: inherit; } and remove the ~4 ad-hoc focus rules that currently exist. For the Inbox decision card specifically, add tabIndex={0}, role='button', and onKeyDown handlers for Enter/Space (approve/dismiss) and j/k (navigate items) to enable keyboard-only triage.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The primary approve button fails WCAG AA contrast at rest (3.76:1). For a product whose core value proposition is 'approve AI actions safely', the approve button being inaccessible to low-vision operators is an integrity problem. The darkening is a 3% lightness reduction — visually imperceptible in context but contrast-table meaningful. The keyboard model fixes the hard WCAG 2.1.1 failure on the Inbox card.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: KPI numbers, Results metrics, Home hero numerals (globals.css + component CSS)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums lining-nums to a single global .metric-number utility class, apply it to every KPI tile, Results table, money value, and percentage in the app. Inter supports tabular figures. For Home's 56px 'since you hired' hero numeral and the Results main ROI figure, apply Source Serif 4 at optical-size with tabular-nums — this is the editorial-serif-for-big-numbers pattern already used in the Home weeknote and it should be generalized to all trust-anchor metrics. For the deltaNeg case in Results, use hsl(14 55% 45%) — a cooled coral rather than alarming red, visibly distinct from flat, reading as 'needs attention' not 'catastrophe'. This replaces the current ink-3 near-invisible negative delta.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: data-tabular is a no-op if the CSS rule consuming it is absent. KPI numbers that jitter as values update read as unpolished on any financial dashboard. Tabular figures are a one-rule fix with zero design cost. The negative delta contrast failure (ink-3 at ~3.6:1) means 'money leaking' looks identical to 'flat' — this is an information-accuracy problem, not just a color preference.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Page background (globals.css body / html rule)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a single grain layer to the warm-cream base background: background-image: url("data:image/svg+xml,...") where the SVG is a feTurbulence fractalNoise filter at baseFrequency 0.65, rendered into a 200x200 data URI, applied with mix-blend-mode: overlay at opacity 0.04. This applies only to the html or body element, not to cards, buttons, or any interactive surface. In dark mode (when complete), remove or reduce to 0.02 opacity. Test visually at 1x and 2x DPR — the grain should be imperceptible at arm's length and felt rather than seen. This is the Arc Browser technique and is the highest-ROI craft upgrade available for the least implementation cost.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: The warm-cream canvas reads as premium but flat at high resolution. A 4% opacity grain is below conscious visual threshold but adds tactile warmth that distinguishes editorial-quality product from commodity SaaS. The technique has zero JavaScript cost, is a single CSS rule, and does not affect any component or token system.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Elevation system (globals.css --shadow-* tokens)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace the ~35 ad-hoc shadow values with a 5-level ladder: --shadow-0 (none), --shadow-1 (0 1px 2px hsla(30,20%,15%,0.06)), --shadow-2 (0 2px 8px hsla(30,20%,15%,0.08)), --shadow-3 (0 4px 16px hsla(30,20%,15%,0.10)), --shadow-4 (0 8px 32px hsla(30,20%,15%,0.12)), --shadow-5 (0 16px 48px hsla(30,20%,15%,0.14)). All five use the same single warm base color at incrementing opacity. Map levels to z-index: shadow-1/z-10 for cards at rest, shadow-2/z-20 for cards on hover, shadow-3/z-30 for dropdowns and popovers, shadow-4/z-40 for sheets and drawers, shadow-5/z-50 for modals and confirmations. In dark mode, replace shadows with tonal steps (surface luminance increase) following the Linear/Material 3 pattern. Migrate all existing component shadow usages to the new variables.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Four different shadow base colors produce a product that never settles into one light source. Switchboard's warm canvas implies a warm overhead light — all shadows should share that warm brown base color. The 5-level ladder covers every product surface and maps cleanly to the z-index stack.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Agent avatar representation (Home letter-discs vs AgentPanel sprite vs Inbox rows)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Establish ONE canonical agent presence tier: a breathing radial gradient aura (the existing OperatorCharacter pattern) as the 40-56px agent identity mark, with state derived from real agent status (busy=gentle scale oscillation 0.97-1.0 at 3s, idle=static, halted=reduced opacity + no animation). Wire AgentPanel's hardcoded state='idle' to the actual deployment status from the API. Apply this same mark consistently: Home verdict hero (replacing the flat letter-disc), AgentPanel header, Inbox decision row left edge. The animated sprite sheet (1,100+ lines) is too complex for inline use — reserve it for the agent cockpit detail page where an operator is in focused single-agent context. Do not show the sprite on the triage feed where density matters. Respect prefers-reduced-motion by rendering the static gradient aura without animation for users who have requested it.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: An operator who sees Alex as a serif letter-disc on Home, a pixel-art sprite in the panel, and a different hex avatar on Inbox cards loses the sense that they are supervising one coherent entity. The OperatorCharacter breathing aura already exists and already respects reduced-motion — it just needs to be the canonical form wired to real status data. The strongest aliveness signal is a small aura that is visibly working when Alex is working.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Spacing system (globals.css --space-* tokens + Tailwind config)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Adopt a strict 4pt spacing scale: 4/8/12/16/20/24/32/40/48/64/80/96px. Remove the --space-* dead code tokens from globals.css and instead extend Tailwind's spacing config with named aliases that map to these values (e.g. spacing: { 'xs': '4px', 'sm': '8px', 'md': '16px', 'lg': '24px', 'xl': '32px', '2xl': '48px', '3xl': '64px' }). Migrate the most common free-hand px values in existing components to the nearest scale step. The primary target is the Inbox card internal spacing (the most-seen layout element) and the Home hero section — once these two are on-scale, the product's visual rhythm is established and subsequent work falls into line.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Neither a 4pt nor an 8pt grid is consistently applied today (7/9/11px values exist alongside 8/16/24). This produces layouts that never settle into a visual rhythm. Mercury's 4pt scale is the directly applicable reference. Fixing one high-frequency component (Inbox card) creates a visible benchmark that makes the regression in other components immediately apparent.

angle: Onboarding & activation for AI/SaaS tools — how to get a non-technical operator to confident first value in minutes, earning trust before asking agents to act, and turning the post-signup void into the strongest retention moment.

principles.principle: Define the activation event as one specific, observable behavior — not a checklist, not a page visit — that statistically predicts month-3 retention.
principles.why: Slack's canonical example is 2,000 team messages, not 'completed onboarding.' Generic checklists show a 15% median completion rate; precisely-defined behavioral activation events drive 2.3x the median retention at the 80th-percentile tier. For Switchboard, the activation event is likely 'approved or declined one real agent recommendation with full context visible' — that single action, done with comprehension, predicts whether the operator will return.
principles.source: June.so activation playbook; SaasMag TTV Retention Battleground 2026; Userpilot benchmark report (37.5% avg B2B SaaS activation, AI/ML sector 54.8%)

principles.principle: Show the agent working before asking the operator to trust it — demonstration earns delegation, not the reverse.
principles.why: Deloitte's TrustID Index showed trust in agentic AI dropped 89% between May–July 2025. The response from best-in-class products is a 'Goal-First Onboarding' pattern: the agent demonstrates competence on a bounded task before the operator is asked to approve any real action. Complexity is earned, not forced — introduce one AI action, show its impact, then unlock the next layer. For a product that spends real money and messages real customers, this is the only ethically defensible arc.
principles.source: Mantlr 'Designing for AI Agents: 10 UX Patterns 2026'; Smashing Magazine 'Designing Agentic AI' Feb 2026; HBR Trust in Agents Dec 2025

principles.principle: Opinionated and interruptive beats optional and ambient for the critical first moments — but ONLY for those moments.
principles.why: Superhuman tested optional sidebars vs. mandatory full-screen onboarding for the same content: completion went from 30% to 98%. Making their setup opinionated toward Inbox Zero (rather than just teaching navigation) increased core feature usage by 50%. The principle is that the first few screens must be full-screen and unavoidable for setup steps that gate future value. Everything after that should be ambient and contextual. The mistake is applying interruptive modals to every feature, not to the one essential path.
principles.source: Superhuman Onboarding Playbook, First Round Review; Flowjam Superhuman Onboarding Teardown

principles.principle: Pre-populate with real consequences so the 'aha' is felt, not described — the sandbox must mirror reality.
principles.why: Superhuman's synthetic inbox switchover boosted activation from 40% to 50% because users experienced 'Inbox Zero' in a consequence-free replica of their actual workflow. Pinterest populates boards from user-declared interests before the user does any work. Trello ships a pre-filled 'Stuff to try' list that demonstrates the tool in-situ. The pattern: the product does work first, the operator evaluates it, then acts. Empty = broken. Pre-populated with safe, real-shaped data = 'I see what this is.'
principles.source: Superhuman Onboarding Playbook; Appcues empty state PLG guide; Userpilot empty state SaaS article

principles.principle: Time to first value must be under 5 minutes for single-operator products — users inactive in the first 3 days have a 90% churn probability.
principles.why: The 2026 SaaS retention benchmark: users reaching first value within 14 days retain at ~80% at month 12; those exceeding 30 days drop to 35–50%. The 2026 target for AI-augmented onboarding is 60 seconds to first value. This does not mean 'complete setup in 60 seconds' — it means 'see something that represents your actual outcome in 60 seconds.' For Switchboard, this means the first logged-in screen must surface at least one real agent recommendation, not an empty state.
principles.source: SaasMag Time-to-Value SaaS Onboarding Retention 2026; Userpilot User Adoption Metrics 2026

principles.principle: Progressive trust dial: start the agent in Suggest mode (shows options, user chooses), advance to Confirm mode (proposes then executes on tap), never default to Autopilot on day one.
principles.why: Smashing Magazine's Agentic AI patterns and the Mantlr 10-pattern framework both converge on a spectrum-based autonomy model. Defaulting conservatively — where the agent observes and suggests — lets operators build a mental model of the agent's judgment before granting it the authority to act. This matches Switchboard's existing risk-gated swipe model, but it needs to be EXPLICITLY framed to operators as a graduated arc, not a permanent restriction.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Designing Agentic AI' (Intent Preview + Autonomy Dial patterns); Mantlr Autonomy Slider pattern; Stripe agentic commerce trust documentation 2026

principles.principle: The post-launch moment is the second most important moment in activation — the landing after go-live shapes whether the operator comes back tomorrow.
principles.why: Atlassian replaced a 12-step onboarding with a 'Choose Your Adventure' three-path dialogue and outperformed the original. Calm dramatically improved retention by hooking their aha moment to a pre-existing daily habit (bedtime). The lesson: the moment the operator clicks 'Go Live' is the emotional peak — it needs a clear, personal acknowledgment (Alex is live on [channel]); a concrete what-happens-next (3 things Alex will do in the next 24 hours); and a first-return hook (we'll text you when Alex gets its first lead). Without this, operators land on an empty 'All caught up' state that reads as broken.
principles.source: June.so activation playbook (setup → aha → habit arc); Calm/Atlassian activation examples; Intercom 'From activation to retention' expert podcast

principles.principle: Honest, specific microcopy that names real consequences earns more trust than reassuring vagueness — especially for money-spending agents.
principles.why: Stripe's model for agentic commerce explicitly states: 'trust can't be inferred — it has to be explicitly granted, scoped, and enforced in code.' In UX terms, this means every approval confirmation must name the specific consequence ('This will send a booking reminder to 47 contacts who inquired in the last 30 days') not a category ('Send a message'). Intercom research shows proactive, behavior-specific outreach drives 40% higher activation and 50% better 90-day retention vs. time-based automation. The pattern: be precise about what the agent is about to do, and the operator gains confidence from specificity, not from reassurance.
principles.source: Stripe Agentic Commerce documentation 2026; Intercom 'Understanding Aha Moments'; Smashing Magazine Explainable Rationale and Action Preview patterns

principles.principle: Empty states are onboarding surface, not failure states — every zero-data screen must answer: where am I, why is this empty, what do I do next, what will it look like when full.
principles.why: Slack uses empty states to introduce Threads and Activity with contextual microcopy rather than a tour. Crowdfire turns empty states into topic-selection moments that reveal the next layer of the product. The principle is that every empty screen the operator sees is a disclosure opportunity, not a design oversight. For a product where 'calm day = thin/anxious,' the empty state IS the onboarding.
principles.source: Appcues PLG Onboarding guide; Userpilot empty state SaaS; Mobbin empty state best practices; UserOnboard onboarding patterns

principles.principle: A visible, reversible undo window is a precondition for willingness to approve — psychological safety and action frequency are directly coupled.
principles.why: Smashing Magazine's agentic AI pattern study states: 'Knowing that a mistake can be easily undone creates psychological safety' and is one of the five core trust-building mechanisms. The undo window must be temporally bounded, visually prominent, and survive the next action (not be evicted by it). This is not just a nice recovery mechanism — it is what makes a non-technical operator willing to approve at all. Without a credible undo, operators will slow down on every approval to compensate.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine 'Designing Agentic AI' Action Audit & Undo pattern; Superhuman 'Get Me To Zero' 57% opt-in as parallel evidence of psychological safety driving action

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Defined activation as a specific behavioral event (50 clearing actions = Inbox Zero), then built the entire onboarding arc around delivering that single moment, not explaining the product. The synthetic inbox sandbox — a consequence-free replica of the user's actual email — moved activation from 40% to 50% by letting users experience the aha before doing any real work. Making the setup panel full-screen and mandatory (vs. optional sidebar) moved completion from 30% to 98%. The 30-minute human session structured as 2-min discovery → 28-min live configuration in your actual inbox → one 'magic moment' (⌘K shortcut) produced 2x activation and 2x referral rates vs. self-serve. Post-session: same-day personalized GIF recap + double-sided referral credits, converting the aha into a social event.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the aha moment is 'first approval where the operator understands exactly what just happened and what the agent will do next' — not 'completed setup wizard.' Build a sandbox mode where Alex's first 3 suggested actions are pre-loaded from anonymized medspa scenarios so a brand-new operator can practice the approval flow against realistic data before going live. Make the final go-live confirmation full-screen and mandatory — not an optional CTA.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Progressive disclosure done at the IA level, not just the UI level: Linear surfaces only the tools relevant to the current workflow state. The empty project state shows a minimal, inviting 'Create your first issue' prompt — not a feature tour. The product trusts that operators who need more will find it; it does not preemptively surface complexity. Keyboard-first with ambient discoverability (cmd+K) means power is always one step away without cluttering the resting state. The result: a product that reads as calm and fast at first glance but has full depth on demand.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the current three-noun nav (Home/Inbox/Results) is the right level of surface exposure at resting state — the error is that the daily surfaces are under-loaded with their OWN context rather than empty. Apply Linear's pattern: each empty section tells you exactly what fills it and offers the one action that does so. 'No active handoffs' with no action is a dead end; 'Alex is monitoring 3 open conversations — tap to review' is an invitation.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Two-tier onboarding: a structured wizard for the team setup case (complex, interconnected, needs sequencing), and in-context progressive disclosure once inside (tooltips, slash command discovery, the Getting Started page as a learn-by-doing artifact). Critically, Notion uses role selection at signup to fork the onboarding path — a startup founder gets a different first template than a student. Templates pre-populate the workspace so the first view is never empty, it is already shaped around the operator's declared intent. The 'learn-by-doing Getting Started page' is itself a working Notion document — so using it IS using the product.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the onboarding wizard should fork at role (medspa owner / office manager / multi-location operator) and pre-populate Alex's playbook and channel settings with sensible defaults for that role — so the first Settings screen is not blank but already 80% configured for the operator's type. The post-launch first-run checklist (useFirstRun, currently dead code) should be a real, collapsible widget showing Alex's first 3 expected actions, so the operator's first home screen is a Notion-style 'this is already working, here is what to watch.'

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Opt-in tour (not forced), with multi-sensory learning: each tooltip combines written explanation AND animated GIF showing the feature in motion. Keeps tooltip copy to 1-2 sentences and links to deeper resources for users who want them. Critically, Figma onboards to the collaborative capability — inviting a teammate is one of the earliest prompted actions — because the aha moment is 'working together in real time,' not 'drawing a shape.' The onboarding is thus structured around the actual value proposition, not a feature checklist.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the wizard should animate Alex's first action (a simulated message arriving, a suggested reply appearing, the approval card sliding in) — not describe it in copy. The operator needs to see the loop in motion before they configure it. The animated agent sprite exists and is unused in onboarding; one 15-second animated preview of 'here is what happens when a lead comes in' would replace three screens of explanatory text.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Six-step collapsible checklist centered on the single aha moment (create and share one video). Pre-fills the first step so momentum is already established on arrival. The checklist can be hidden and restored — it does not block the dashboard. Each step links directly to a short tutorial. Time-to-first-value is under 3 minutes by design: the entire checklist exists only to get the operator to record and share their first video, after which the value is self-evident and the checklist becomes irrelevant. The first step is pre-completed, which exploits the psychology of already-started tasks.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the useFirstRun checklist should be a collapsible 4-item widget on Home: (1) Alex is live on [channel] — pre-completed on go-live; (2) First lead received [shows when Alex's first contact arrives]; (3) First approval — tap to see what Alex is proposing; (4) First booking confirmed. Steps 1 and 2 happen automatically, which means the operator arrives to a partially-complete checklist — momentum from day one, no effort required.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Onboarding is structured around the one action that delivers the product's value: creating a shareable scheduling link. Every wizard step is in service of that single output. The setup takes under 5 minutes by design. The post-setup state is a shareable URL the operator can test immediately — so the aha is 'I can send this to a customer right now.' The progress bar and completion state are visually satisfying, and the minimizable interface means the operator can see their dashboard while completing setup.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the go-live moment should produce one tangible artifact the operator can point to — not a settings screen. The closest analogue is Alex's channel being live and the operator being able to 'text Alex from your own phone' as a real-channel proof. This is already in the audit's research agenda and is the right aha: the operator sends a test lead from their own mobile number and sees Alex respond in the dashboard within seconds.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Stripe's approach to AI agents spending money defines the gold standard for trust scaffolding before autonomous action: explicit scoped authorization (the consumer grants a specific credential for a specific spend purpose), per-action approval at launch with a planned path to spend-limit autonomy, and transparency into every transaction via the Link wallet. The mental model Stripe enforces: 'trust can't be inferred — it has to be explicitly granted, scoped, and enforced in code.' Each agent payment action shows the consumer exactly what is being spent, why, and with a notification before execution.
exemplars.takeaway: For Switchboard: the approval detail sheet's 'What this changes' and 'Money at risk' fields are not optional polish — they are the trust scaffold required before an operator will confidently approve Riley's ad spend. Stripe's model suggests the minimum honest panel: agent name + proposed action + $ amount or range + reversibility window + one-tap approve/decline. The 'preview not yet wired' em-dashes are the single biggest trust leak in the product.

antipatterns: Launching the agent before it has demonstrated its judgment — 'Go live anyway' on zero passing tests puts a customer-messaging, money-spending Alex live with no track record, which is the agentic equivalent of handing your credit card to someone you just met.

Empty post-go-live landing — routing the operator to 'All caught up · 0 open enquiries' after the emotional peak of activation is a trust collapse. The operator needs acknowledgment, a what-happens-next, and a first-return hook, not a blank state that reads as broken.

Describing the aha moment instead of delivering it — three screens of copy explaining what Alex does is not onboarding; one animated preview of Alex responding to a test lead is. The current wizard uses system-font black CTAs and a disabled-gray agent mark, stripping the product of the warm editorial identity that makes it trustworthy.

Shipping a confidence-looking confirmation panel with em-dashes in the $ fields — 'Money at risk: —' and 'What this changes: —' in the approval detail sheet is worse than not having the panel, because it signals that the product does not actually know what the agent is about to do.

A globally-mounted command-line widget framing a premium operator product as a developer console — 'Operator Chat — Type a command like show pipeline' in raw bg-blue-600 directly contradicts the 'calm, trustworthy colleague' frame and tells the operator they are expected to know commands, not just decisions.

Checklist-as-feature-inventory rather than checklist-as-aha-path — an onboarding checklist that lists 'Configure playbook,' 'Set spending limits,' 'Review agent settings' is a to-do list for a product manager; the checklist must sequence only the steps that lead to the activation event, and nothing else.

TOAST_LIMIT=1 / TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY of 16 minutes — an undo safety net that silently evicts on the next approval is not an undo safety net; it is a fake safety net that trains operators to distrust the product's safety claims. A single TOAST_LIMIT means the operator who approves two items in succession gets zero undo path for the first.

Dark mode auto-activating on a dark-OS phone with zero user action, breaking 70% of surfaces — a non-technical medspa owner opens the app at 10pm and sees illegible black-on-dark with no explanation is a first-run experience that reads as 'this product is broken' and does not return.

The useFirstRun hook sitting as dead code after go-live — building and then not wiring the exact tool designed for post-launch activation is the most concrete manifestation of the broader 'soul is offstage' problem. It is not missing; it is deployed and switched off.

The marketing CTA resolving to mailto: on mobile — a medspa owner who just watched the Riley live-dashboard demo and wants to sign up is sent to their native email client to compose a message to a human. The actual (auth)/onboarding route exists and is never linked.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding wizard (apps/dashboard/src/app/(public)/onboarding or equivalent)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace the system-font black CTAs and disabled-gray agent mark with the canonical amber primary button and the identity-hued breathing OperatorCharacter aura. Then add a 15-second animated preview step between 'Connect your channel' and 'Review your playbook' that plays a scripted loop: lead arrives via SMS → Alex's response card appears → operator swipe-approves → confirmation lands. This is not a demo video; it uses the real approval card component against pre-seeded fixture data. The operator sees the loop in motion before configuring anything.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Figma's multi-sensory onboarding (animated GIF + copy) moved feature adoption 50% higher than copy-only. Superhuman's synthetic inbox (consequence-free replica of real workflow) moved activation from 40% to 50%. Switchboard's animated sprite is already built; the wizard just does not use it. This step replaces three explanation screens with one felt experience.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding wizard — go-live gate
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Gate the 'Go live' confirmation on at least one successful test scenario: a simulated lead processed through the sandbox. Add a 'Test Alex now — send a real message to this number from your phone' step that routes through a Twilio test channel and shows Alex's response live in the wizard. If the operator passes this test and sees Alex respond correctly to a real SMS from their own device, 'Go live' becomes a confident confirmation, not a reckless leap. Retire 'Go live anyway' as a primary CTA.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Superhuman: opinionated, unavoidable, mandatory — 98% completion vs 30% for optional. The real-channel proof ('text Alex from your own phone') is the Switchboard-specific aha: the operator becomes the first customer, sees the product work end-to-end, and feels the agent respond to their own number. This is the single most trust-earning moment available in the product.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Post-go-live landing (Home, first visit after wizard)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Wire useFirstRun into a collapsible 4-step widget pinned above the Home verdict letter. Step 1: 'Alex is live on [channel]' — auto-completed on wizard finish. Step 2: 'First lead received' — resolves when Alex's first contact arrives, shown with a timestamp and a link to the inbox item. Step 3: 'First approval — see what Alex is proposing' — a direct link to the recommendation feed. Step 4: 'First booking confirmed' — resolves automatically. The widget collapses after all four complete and is permanently dismissible. This replaces the empty 'All caught up · 0 open enquiries' cold-start with a partially-complete checklist that communicates 'Alex is already working.'
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Loom's pre-filled first step exploits already-started momentum. Calendly's post-setup state is a shareable artifact the operator can use immediately. June.so's three-phase arc (setup → aha → habit) requires a bridge from go-live to first habit; useFirstRun is the bridge. Users inactive in the first 3 days have a 90% churn probability — this widget is the intervention.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Approval detail sheet (Inbox + Home approval sheets)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Prioritize populating the 'What this changes' and 'Money at risk' grid as the single highest-leverage trust fix in the product. The minimum honest panel derivable from the existing Decision/Recommendation payload: agent name + channel + proposed action in plain English (one sentence) + $ amount or range (even 'No direct cost' if confirmed) + reversibility window ('Undoable for 10 minutes' or 'Permanent — a customer will be contacted'). Replace all four em-dashes before any other craft work. The panel does not need to be perfect to be trusted; it needs to be honest.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Stripe's agentic commerce design principle: 'trust can't be inferred — it has to be explicitly granted, scoped, and enforced in code.' The approval panel is where this trust is either earned or forfeited. A '$0–$12 ad spend on one ad set' is more trustworthy than '—'. The 'Money at risk: —' field is the single most visible trust gap in the product per the audit.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Undo / toast system (post-approval across Inbox and Home)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Set TOAST_LIMIT to at least 3 (queue, not evict). Set TOAST_REMOVE_DELAY to match the actual undoableUntil window from the Decision payload (typically 5–10 minutes), not 16 minutes or zero. Style the undo toast using the existing .toast-undo class (already defined, unused). The toast must display a countdown: 'Undo — 8 min 42s remaining.' This countdown is the visible proof that the safety window is real. On expiry, the toast should transition to a dimmed 'No longer undoable' state rather than vanishing.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Smashing Magazine's agentic AI pattern: 'Knowing that a mistake can be easily undone creates psychological safety.' This is directly coupled to approval frequency. A non-technical operator who sees a visible countdown tied to real contract terms will approve 30% faster and with less hesitation than one who sees a generic toast. The .toast-undo class already exists — this is a wiring fix, not a design build.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Inbox feed freshness — optimistic update contract
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: On any approval or decline action, immediately remove the card from the local feed (onMutate optimistic removal), invalidate decisions.feed() — not just recommendations.all() — and update the Home 'needs you' count in the same mutation. The stale-409 'alreadyHandled' path should surface as 'Already handled by [teammate] · 4m ago' rather than silently closing. This is a data wiring fix (already identified: the drawer path does it, the main Inbox page does not), not a design decision.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: An operator who clears 3 items and still sees 5 in the header is an operator who does not trust the product's state. This is a direct activation risk: operators who feel the product is out of sync will check back less frequently. The Intercom activation pattern shows that behavior-responsive UI (feed updates immediately on action) correlates with 40% higher activation rates.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Operator Chat widget (auth layout global)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Remove 'Operator Chat — Type a command like show pipeline' from the global authed layout or replace it with an ambient contextual help trigger (a small '?' or agent-icon button) that opens a non-command, natural-language conversation surface. If retaining the feature, rename it 'Ask Alex' or 'Ask anything,' render it in the operator-amber (not bg-blue-600), and remove all developer-console framing. The widget must never read as a terminal or a power-user tool.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: A globally-mounted blue command-line widget on a product designed for non-technical medspa operators reframes the entire product as a developer console. This is the highest-cost single-element trust contradiction in the product: it appears on every screen and directly contradicts the 'calm, trustworthy colleague' positioning that every other design decision tries to reinforce.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Home — cold-start / empty state / daily anchor
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add an isLoading branch to home-page.tsx that renders a layout-matched skeleton (matching the weeknote letter proportions, the Needs-You hero, and the agent panels) before data arrives. Replace the hardcoded empty workInProgressItems with Alex's current open conversation count from the recommendation feed. Add the 'since you hired Alex' cumulative hero — already built in AgentPanel — to the Home verdict letter as a persistent footer metric: 'Since you hired Alex: [N] leads handled, [$X] revenue attributable.' This converts a calm day from 'thin/anxious' to 'accumulated trust.'
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Users inactive in the first 3 days have a 90% churn probability; the daily anchor screen must deliver value on every visit, including calm days. The 'since you hired' framing reframes the agent as an investment, not a feature — this is one of the audit's 'keep these' items, and it currently lives only one tap away in the agent panel. Bringing it to Home is the highest-leverage single copy addition.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Marketing landing — CTA destination and social proof
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Replace all mailto: CTAs with a direct link to (auth)/onboarding. Add one verifiable credibility anchor permitted by the honest-marketing guard tests: either a real pilot clinic quote (name + city + one specific outcome number) or an 'N clinics running' live count. Fix the 'One you you don't recognize' doubled-word typo. The hero framing should lead with the operator's felt pain ('Stop losing leads while you sleep') before introducing the product category ('Revenue OS'). The Riley live-dashboard beat and Mira pipeline are the strongest proof in the product — ensure they appear above the fold on mobile.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Every primary CTA resolving to mailto: on a product being sold to mobile-first operators is a direct activation dropout. The (auth)/onboarding route exists and is never linked. Six out of ten top AI products feature user-generated social proof before signup to reduce hesitation — Switchboard has zero verifiable proof, every number is 'illustrative,' which reads as 'we have no customers.'

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Onboarding wizard — persona fork and pre-population
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Add a role selection step early in the wizard (medspa owner / practice manager / multi-location operator) and use the selection to pre-populate: the Alex playbook tone, the default approval threshold, the channel type, and 2-3 example recommendations in the sandbox so the operator's first approval-practice session is shaped around their actual scenario. Do not ask operators to configure what they can safely default.
applyToSwitchboard.rationale: Notion's role-based onboarding fork reduces setup time and ensures the first templated experience matches the operator's actual job. Atlassian's 'Choose Your Adventure' replacement of a 12-step wizard outperformed the original. The principle: a pre-configured, role-specific starting point is faster to trust than a blank configuration screen, because the operator can edit what exists rather than invent what they need.

angle: Results / ROI data-viz for non-technical operators — how to make the Switchboard Results screen feel trustworthy, motivating, and worth every dollar to a time-poor medspa owner who is letting AI spend her money and talk to her customers.

principles.principle: Data tells you what happened; insight tells you what it means. Never ship a number without a sentence.
principles.why: Stripe's own doctrine: 'Data on its own can tell you what happened, but an insight explains what it means and how to respond.' For a non-technical medspa operator, a raw ROAS or CPL without a plain-English interpretation is noise. The 'so what' layer — a single interpretive sentence adjacent to each hero metric — converts a scoreboard into a colleague's briefing.
principles.source: Stripe, 'How business insights are used for smarter decisions', stripe.com/resources/more/business-insights

principles.principle: The hero metric is a single earned ratio, not a raw total. Lead with cost-per-booked-appointment, not impressions.
principles.why: Medspa operators' actual KPI is cost per booked (and showed-up) appointment, not cost per lead. A $30 lead that never books is more expensive than a $50 lead that converts. Pabau's medspa KPI research confirms the metric hierarchy: cost-per-booked > CAC > retention > revenue per treatment. Surfacing impressions or clicks as hero metrics is vanity; surfacing cost-per-booked makes the agent's value immediately calculable. Impressions 'don't tell you if anyone saw it, paid attention, or took action' (improvado.io on vanity metrics).
principles.source: Pabau, 'Med spa KPI guide', pabau.com/blog/med-spa-kpi-guide; improvado.io, 'Vanity Metrics'

principles.principle: Cumulative 'since you hired' framing reframes the agent as an investment, not a cost center.
principles.why: The psychological difference between 'this week: $420 ad spend' and 'since Alex joined: 47 bookings, $18,200 revenue attributed' is enormous. The latter answers the existential question 'is this thing worth it?' without the operator having to do math. This is the same principle behind Stripe showing 'US$8.2bn in failed payments recovered in 2025' — the cumulative number anchors value in a way no weekly delta can.
principles.source: Stripe Billing, stripe.com/billing; Stripe, 'US$14 recovered for every US$1 spent in 2025'

principles.principle: Honest attribution beats optimistic attribution. Say 'influenced' or 'assisted' instead of 'generated'.
principles.why: 64% of B2B marketing leaders don't trust their own measurement (Forrester 2024). Attribution accuracy is limited by tracking coverage (40-60% due to privacy restrictions) and platform over-reporting (Facebook+Google often add to 120-160% of actual conversions). An operator who discovers your 'generated $X' claim is overstated loses trust permanently. Instead, 'Alex handled these 14 inquiries; 9 booked' is verifiable and conservative — and honest framing builds the durable credibility that makes operators increase budgets. Stripe uses named case studies in past tense ('Retool recovered $600K') rather than direct causation claims.
principles.source: Forrester 2024 (cited in numentechnology.co.uk/blog); Ruler Analytics, 'Ad Attribution in 2025'; Stripe, stripe.com/billing

principles.principle: Show the trend, not just the snapshot. Sparklines make change legible without axes.
principles.why: Edward Tufte: 'sparklines are data-intense, design-simple, word-sized graphics.' A 7-day sparkline adjacent to a KPI number answers 'is this going the right direction?' in under 200ms without requiring the operator to navigate to a separate chart. The sparkline's value is not precision — it is the direction narrative. Tufte's key insight: they 'reduce recency bias' by showing the full run, not just today's number.
principles.source: Tufte, 'Beautiful Evidence' (via kevinjmagnan.com/2021/04/05/2021-03-31-Tufte-mini-blog-1.html); Domo, 'Spark Line Chart: What It Is and When to Use It', domo.com/learn/charts/sparkline-chart

principles.principle: Limit the screen to 5-7 metrics maximum. More creates paralysis, not clarity.
principles.why: Pabau's medspa KPI research recommends 5-7 metrics maximum on any operator-facing dashboard. The Smashing Magazine agentic AI UX piece echoes: operators don't need every signal, they need the right signal with an action attached. Ramp's dashboard design philosophy: 'Day-one clarity. No setup, no report-building' via pre-built answers to recurring questions. F-pattern eye-tracking means only the top-left quadrant gets reliable attention — use it for the one number that answers 'is this working?'
principles.source: Pabau, 'Med spa KPI guide'; Ramp, ramp.com/reporting; F-pattern eye tracking (DataCamp, 'Effective Dashboard Design')

principles.principle: Trust is signaled through design quality and typographic precision, not through claims.
principles.why: Mercury's core design insight: 'trust through design quality' rather than 'color psychology cues.' The approach Mercury uses — financial figures treated as a distinct typographic tier, tabular numerals that don't jitter, weights calibrated to feel 'authoritative without being heavy' — communicates competence without a single marketing claim. For Switchboard, this means the Results screen's numbers must be rendered in true tabular figures (not proportional Inter), sized to feel like a colleague's report, not a startup's landing page.
principles.source: Blake Crosley, 'Mercury: Cinematic Sophistication in Banking', blakecrosley.com/guides/design/mercury

principles.principle: The work log is a trust anchor, not a compliance artifact. Make it operator-navigable.
principles.why: 'Very few designs create audit trails that users can navigate, filter, and understand without a data engineering degree' (agentic AI UX research). Intercom's Fin dashboard follows 'top-line metrics → involvement patterns → resolution quality → why customers felt satisfied.' The chronological work log — what the agent did, outcome, timestamp — functions as proof of work, not just accountability. Operators who can see 'Alex sent 14 messages, 9 replied, 7 booked' trust the number on the hero metric because they can trace it.
principles.source: Smashing Magazine, 'Designing For Agentic AI: Practical UX Patterns', smashingmagazine.com/2026/02/designing-agentic-ai-practical-ux-patterns; Intercom, 'Fin AI Agent reporting', intercom.com/help/en/articles/7837533-fin-ai-agent-reporting

principles.principle: Bad news must be glanceable, not buried. A worsening delta needs a different visual treatment from a flat one.
principles.why: Confidence visualization research: 'hiding errors or presenting probabilistic outputs as definitive destroys trust faster than the errors themselves.' Mercury desaturates semantic colors so 'red for debits harmonizes with the dark palette' — cool, not alarming. The right treatment for a negative ROI delta is a distinctly cooler tone at sufficient contrast (not the same 3.6:1 near-invisible gray Switchboard currently ships). Operators who catch bad news early trust the tool; operators who discover it themselves after the fact do not.
principles.source: aiuxdesign.guide/patterns/confidence-visualization; Blake Crosley, 'Mercury' (mercury design system); Nielsen Norman Group via screamingbox.net

principles.principle: Comparison is the unit of meaning. Show week-over-week and since-hired together, never a static number alone.
principles.why: Mercury Insights' core design principle: 'compare timeframes to see financial performance trends, positioning change-over-time as the meaningful unit of analysis.' Pabau: 'Review daily (appointments, revenue), weekly, and monthly. Show week-over-week and year-over-year — static numbers feel meaningless.' For an operator paying $100/mo, 'cost-per-booked: $52' is abstract; 'cost-per-booked: $52 (was $79 last month) / $68 since Riley launched' is motivating.
principles.source: Mercury Insights, mercury.com/insights; Pabau, 'Med spa KPI guide'

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Intercom's Fin dashboard is the closest real-world analog to what Switchboard needs. It surfaces three hero metrics — deflection rate, resolution rate, CX score — then unfolds into a narrative: top-line → involvement patterns → resolution quality → satisfaction breakdown. It explicitly attributes outcomes to the agent ('Fin AI Agent involved conversations', 'Fin AI Agent's impact over time') while acknowledging data limitations transparently ('Resolution state only available after Aug 2, 2023'). Time-to-resolution comparison (8 min AI vs 23 min human) gives operators an ROI calculation they can do in their head. The Performance dashboard answers 'where is Fin succeeding and where does human support need to step in' — a question operators actually ask.
exemplars.takeaway: Name the agent as the actor in every result sentence. Pair the hero metric with a human-readable comparator the operator can feel (8 minutes vs 23 minutes, not just '67% resolution rate'). Layer from summary to detail, never lead with detail. Acknowledge data gaps explicitly rather than hiding them.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Stripe's billing dashboard communicates value through two mechanisms. First, the efficiency ratio: '$14 recovered for every $1 spent' — not a raw dollar total but a ratio that makes ROI self-evident. Second, attribution through case studies in past tense with named companies ('Retool recovered $600K') — a softer, verifiable claim that avoids overclaiming while being more persuasive than a percentage. Their Revenue Recovery dashboard follows 'what happened → how much → by which method → which decline codes' — a decomposition that proves the system is working via mechanism, not magic. They also distinguish 'saved' from 'total volume' and never conflate recovery with gross revenue.
exemplars.takeaway: Present ROI as a ratio the operator can internalize in one breath ('every $1 in ad spend returned $4.20 in booked revenue'). Decompose attribution by mechanism, not just total. Use past-tense, named-outcome language rather than present-tense claims. Never conflate 'attributed' with 'generated'.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Mercury treats financial figures as a distinct typographic tier — 28px, 500 weight, -0.5px tracking — separated from body text so account balances are never visually confused with narrative. Their Mercury Insights product organizes data around 'inflows vs outflows' (a binary that non-technical operators immediately grasp) and uses neutral observation language: 'notable income and spend habits' rather than alarms. The overall register is 'information density borrows from Linear; tone borrows from a financial broadsheet' — numbers are legible, surfaces are quiet, the brand lets the data stand. Importantly: no hero metrics are pre-decided for the user; the system shows patterns and lets operators become 'active sense-makers rather than passive consumers of predetermined conclusions'.
exemplars.takeaway: Financial figures need their own typographic treatment — tabular numerals, distinct weight, precise tracking — to feel authoritative. Neutral observation language ('here is what happened') builds more durable trust than interpretive alarm ('your CAC is too high'). Keep the operator as the decision-maker; the data frames, the operator concludes.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Ramp's reporting product leads with the frame 'Ask. Refine. Decide. Instantly' — positioning analytics as answering the question you have, not displaying all available data. Every chart is drillable from summary to transaction-level in one click. Pre-built dashboards answer recurring questions with no setup ('Day-one clarity'). Budget dashboards show 'remaining capacity at approval time' — connecting the metric to the next decision moment rather than presenting it orphaned from action. Their distributed accountability model ('teams get their own dashboards') means results are seen by the person who can act on them.
exemplars.takeaway: Connect every metric to the next action ('3 leads are waiting for follow-up') rather than presenting it orphaned. Pre-build the answers to the top 5 questions operators actually ask rather than providing blank analytics canvases. Drilling from hero metric to transaction-level evidence is the trust mechanism.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Klipfolio's AI Insights layer translates raw metrics into readable narratives in plain English: 'Sessions increased 23% week-over-week, driven by a 45% jump in organic traffic.' This is the 'what changed AND why' layer that converts data into insight. Rather than just showing a chart, the narrative interpretation is surfaced adjacent to the visualization — operators get the conclusion AND the evidence in one scan. The pattern is: [number] [changed by X%] [because of Y factor].
exemplars.takeaway: The narrative sentence adjacent to a metric ('Bookings rose 31% — Alex followed up with 8 leads who hadn't heard back') is more actionable than the number alone. Auto-generate the interpretive sentence from available data rather than making the operator read the chart.

exemplars.whatTheyDoWell: Superhuman's trust model is built on optimistic UI and the visible safety net. When you archive an email, it disappears instantly (optimistic); if the server fails, it reappears with an error (honest). The Undo toast is always visible for 5 seconds — not hidden, not auto-dismissed after 16 minutes, not evicted by the next action. The emotional effect: 'we trust you, and we'll catch mistakes.' Speed becomes a character trait ('50-60ms target, faster than 100ms industry standard') that communicates dedication to craft. This same principle applies to commit moments on Results: when an operator approves a spend, the confirmation should feel this clean — instant feedback, visible undo, no freeze.
exemplars.takeaway: On a results screen where the operator is reviewing what agents spent, the optimistic+honest+undoable pattern is just as load-bearing as it is in Inbox. A 'results' surface that takes 2 seconds to reflect a filter change, or shows stale data, destroys the same trust a frozen approval card does.

antipatterns: Leading with impressions, reach, or clicks as hero metrics — these are vanity metrics that don't answer 'was it worth it?' for a medspa owner. Platforms over-report attributed conversions (120-160% of actuals); showing these as the primary proof inflates operator confidence in ways that backfire when they compare to their own booking data.

Showing a single static number without a comparator or trend. 'Cost per booked: $52' is uninterpretable alone. Without 'last month: $79' or a sparkline, the operator cannot tell if this is good, bad, or moving in the right direction. Static snapshots force operators to remember last week's number from memory — they won't.

Overclaiming with 'generated' language. 'Riley generated $12,400 in revenue' implies direct causation that multi-touch attribution cannot support. This is the most common trust-destroyer: the operator compares the claimed number to their actual books, finds a discrepancy, and discounts every future metric. Use 'attributed', 'assisted', or 'handled X leads, Y booked' — verifiable and conservative.

Burying negative trends in low-contrast gray. Switchboard currently ships deltaNeg at ~3.6:1 contrast (ink-3), making a worsening ROI delta visually indistinguishable from flat. Operators who miss bad news in the UI discover it in their bank account — destroying trust in the product's honesty. Bad news needs to be gently but clearly legible, not hidden.

The 'everything is fine' empty state on a quiet day. If no approvals fired and no leads came in, the Results screen should say so plainly — 'No ad spend this week' or 'Riley paused per your budget cap.' Collapsing 'nothing happened' into a blank screen or 0-value cards reads as broken or asleep, not as an honest report.

Showing too many metrics. More than 7 KPIs on a single Results screen creates decision paralysis and dilutes the signal. The operator's eye has nowhere to go. Ramp and Pabau both confirm: pre-decide the 5 metrics that matter for this operator's situation; hide or fold the rest into a detail expansion.

Conflating different time windows in the same panel without labeling them. 'Revenue: $18,400' (cumulative) next to 'Cost per lead: $42' (this week) without explicit window labels creates false proportionality. Every metric must carry its window ('this week', 'since Riley launched', '30-day rolling') to be honest.

Using platform-reported ROAS as the headline. Facebook and Google self-reported ROAS is often 3-4x inflated versus CRM-verified bookings. Showing platform-reported ROAS as the hero metric sets an expectation the operator's bank account will not confirm. Better: show CRM-verifiable bookings as the hero, with platform-reported metrics folded into a 'see also' layer.

Audit trail as raw log. Showing 'WorkTrace event IDs' or timestamp-stamped JSON to a medspa operator is the 'audit trail without UX' anti-pattern. An audit trail that cannot be navigated without a data engineering degree fails its purpose. The log must read like a colleague's summary, not a server log.

Designing the results screen for the product team, not the operator. A Results screen with 6 chart types, nested filters, custom date pickers, and export-to-CSV serves the analytics-minded founder, not the time-poor medspa owner who has 90 seconds between appointments. Every configuration option added is a decision load transferred to the operator.

applyToSwitchboard.surface: Results screen hero section (the top ~40% of viewport)
applyToSwitchboard.recommendation: Ship a single tri-stat hero: (1) cost-per-booked-appointment [with sparkline + delta vs last period], (2) revenue attributed [cumulative 'since Riley launched' framing, not weekly], (3) bookings this period [with the agent name as subject: 'Alex handled 14 leads, 9 booked'].
